narrow semgrep test and practitioners pinned it on scaffolding, not the base model — directional, not verified. on the markets side the month's repricing whipsawed (jun 22–23 rout on capex/ipo fears → jun 25 micron blowout + morgan stanley 'crowded dip, not broken roi', which resolved our jun 23 capex-roi-defence call ✅).","doit":"treat model availability as a procurement question: scope client work around the generally-available tier (terra/luna-, sonnet-class), keep at least one open-weight model (glm-class) in your fallback list and test it on real tasks now, and design features to degrade gracefully under rate limits and price drift. trust the benchmark you ran, not the headline.","note":"climate signal, not a trade. the open 'dip not regime change' call (due ~jul 20) stays open, leaning held but volatile; the google→meta cap supports the 'compute is the binding constraint' call (due ~aug 3)."}]},{"h":"techniques & tools worth adopting this week — the keepers","intro":"the survivors after killing 95%. each is a run-it-today play with a real link and a built-in guardrail.","blocks":[{"sub":"the defaults","skills → add skill → ds rules as markdown; test with /follow-ds-guidelines"],["figma motion → dev mode","animate on-canvas and copy exact css/react easing (no after effects)","keyframe one hover; copy the css; validate the curve on your front-end"],["gemini 3.5 flash computer use","a cheap agent clicks through gui work that has no api/export","docker sandbox + allowlist + both opt-in safeguards on before any real task"],["ai sdk 7 tool approvals","agents that pause for a human yes/no and survive a restart","toolapproval:'user-approval' on spend/send/delete tools + a spend generative ai report","first-party impressions inside ai overviews/ai mode","performance → generative ai; export + date-stamp a baseline; re-measure weekly"],["standard shopping max-value bidding","bid for revenue without the pmax black box","switch strategy, leave troas empty 2–3 wks, then set troas near achieved roas"],["email sunset flow + dmarcbis np=reject","protect inbox placement for your buyers; close the subdomain spoof hole","behaviour-based win-back→suppress; append np=reject to _dmarc (10-min dns edit)"]]}}]},{"h":"predictions ledger — resolved & newly on the mode/discover; mueller clarified counting jun 25). honest caveat: it had already launched jun 3 (we missed it), and it's impressions-only, not a bing-style citation- share metric — early/partial on this week, still open)","list":[" [web-design] figma agent skills become billable ai-credit actions at ga, and/or a community skills marketplace / cross-tool .md skill standard emerges — due ~end of title: Daily Briefing — Sunday, June 28
q: daily briefing — sunday, june 28 sunday, june 28, 2026 web-dev play of the day: before you let an ai agent buy, send, or delete anything, put a human-approval gate on that one action — and make the agent survive a restart while it waits. ai sdk 7 ships both built-in. on jun 25 2026 vercel released ai sdk 7 (the most-used toolkit for building ai features in javascript apps). two parts are run-it-today. tool approvals: set toolapproval: { deletefile: 'user-approval' } on a risky tool and the agent pauses and asks you before running it (options: ask-a-human / auto-approve / auto-deny / your-own-rule); high-stakes flows can hmac-sign the approval so the tool's arguments can't be swapped between 'approved' and 'run.' durable workflows: build with workflowagent from @ai-sdk/workflow and the agent's progress is saved to storage between steps — a deploy, a crash, or an approval that lands hours later doesn't lose the work; it resumes from where it paused. that's the gap between a demo and something you'd let touch a real customer account. run-it-today: npm i ai@7 (needs node 22+/esm) → npx @ai-sdk/codemod v7 to auto-migrate → wrap actions in toolloopagent → gate the dangerous ones. (on v6? npx skills add vercel/ai --skill migrate-ai-sdk-v6-to-v7 and let your coding agent run it; review against the official migration guide.) card: card-webdev-ai-sdk-7-agents. email play: stop fighting for opens and fix the leak that actually sends you to spam — your dead subscribers. win them back, then sunset the ones who stay asleep. mailbox providers (gmail, yahoo, outlook) decide inbox-vs-spam by watching whether people want your mail; keep blasting a big pool who never open and you teach them it's unwanted — and they start junking it for your buyers too (litmus, jun 2026). the play, run as a standing flow: (1) define 'disengaged' by behavior, not opens — no click, visit, or purchase in 6+ months (apple mail auto-opens make open-rate a lie); (2) send a short re-engagement series (2–3 emails: 'still want these?', a value reminder, a last-chance offer); (3) anyone who clicks/buys → back to active; anyone who ignores it all → suppress them , don't keep mailing; (4) drop hard-bounced/invalid addresses (some are spam traps ). it feels scary to shrink the list on purpose, but a smaller awake list lands in the inbox; a big ignored one doesn't. check the lift in gmail's free postmaster 'deliverability analysis' before/after. card: card-email-reengagement-sunset. paid play (google-ads): you can finally get revenue-optimized shopping bidding without surrendering to performance max. switch standard shopping to 'maximize conversion value.' rolling out june 2026 , google ads lets standard shopping use maximize conversion value bidding without a required target roas — previously, optimizing for revenue (not just cheap clicks) usually meant moving to a feed-only pmax campaign and giving up search-term visibility and control. now you keep the transparent campaign type and bid for total revenue. run-it-today: confirm you're tracking conversion value (revenue per order, not just order count) → campaign settings → bidding → change bid strategy → maximize conversion value → leave the target roas box empty to start → let it learn 2–3 weeks → then add a target roas near your achieved roas to hold efficiency. label heads-up: the old 'maximize conversion value with a target roas' is being renamed simply 'target roas' in june 2026 — same behavior, new name. card: card-paid-standard-shopping-max-value. (corroboration: reported (2) — search engine land + search engine roundtable; google ads help documents the strategy.) also moved — the ai landscape (cross-checked; weekend, us markets closed): with the frontier gated, the air is being filled by open/chinese models and a deepening compute crunch. z.ai's open-weight glm-5.2 drew headlines on jun 28 for reportedly matching top us models at finding security bugs — but read it with the cross-check: the parity claim traces to a narrow semgrep test (≈39% f1 on one bug class vs 32–37% for claude code), and practitioners (incl. vercel's guillermo rauch) pushed back that scaffolding , not the base model, drives those numbers. still directionally real: a freely downloadable model is now close enough that, with the us gating mythos/gpt-5.6, open weights become the pragmatic default for a lot of work (route to it: card-ai-tooling-glm-5-2-route, card-ai-tooling-model-portability). meanwhile compute scarcity bit a giant: google capped meta's use of gemini because it couldn't supply the capacity meta wanted (told meta around march; meta has told staff to use ai tokens more efficiently) — confirmed via bloomberg + ft. and a vivid tell of gating-plus-scarcity: wired reports a thriving underground market for claude access in china , with 'transfer station' sites buying api tokens abroad and reselling them. the throughline for us is unchanged but sharper: the best model may not be in your account on day one — design around the tier you can actually get, and keep an open-weight model in your fallback list. (open calls stay open: the jun 8 'ai selloff is a dip' [due ~jul 20] and 'power/compute is the binding constraint' [due ~aug 3] — the google→meta cap reads as supporting the constraint call; the jun 19 'glm-5.2 reaches openrouter top-3' [due ~jul 19] gains momentum.) sharpen your edge: today's throughline is keep a human hand — and a backup — on anything you don't fully control. ai sdk 7 makes the agent ask before it acts and survive the wait; the email sunset move means you stop letting a dead list quietly control your inbox placement ; standard shopping's new bidding hands you revenue optimization without the pmax black box ; and the landscape says the very best models now arrive gated, so the cheapest insurance is an open-weight fallback you can run yourself. also still true and answered: the recurring cro/aov 'best one thing' ask — don't import a case study, use ai on your own order export + ga4 funnel to name your single biggest $ leak, then pull one lever (card-cro-ai-diagnose-one-lever). approval gates, sunset flows, value bidding, fallback models: four different ways to refuse to fly blind. web-dev email paid news [{"h":"web dev — run it today: gate your ai agent's risky actions behind a human yes/no, and make the agent survive restarts, with ai sdk 7's tool approvals + durable workflows","blocks":[{"sub":"jun 25 2026: vercel shipped ai sdk 7 — the production foundation for agents. two run-it-today parts: tool approvals (the agent pauses for a human before a risky tool runs; policies = user-approval / auto-approve / auto-deny / custom, with optional hmac signing that binds the approval to the exact arguments) and durable workflows (workflowagent from @ai-sdk/workflow persists state between steps, so the agent survives deploys/crashes and approvals that arrive hours later)","tags":["web-dev","ai-tooling","ai-sdk","agents","human-in-the-loop","durable-workflows"],"p":"what changed / the technique: on jun 25 2026 vercel released ai sdk 7 , the most-used javascript/typescript toolkit for adding ai to apps. the headline isn't a new model — it's the plumbing that turns a fragile prototype agent into one you can run in production: reasoning control, runtime/tool context, telemetry, sandbox support, and the two we'd ship first. (1) tool approvals. an agent that can call tools is an agent that can take real actions — and the dangerous ones (charge a card, send an email, delete a record) shouldn't fire on the model's say-so alone. in v7 you attach an approval policy at the call or agent level — toolapproval: { deletefile: 'user-approval' } — and the run pauses and surfaces an approval request (the action + its arguments) for a human to allow or deny. policies can be 'user-approval' , 'auto-approve' , 'auto-deny' , or a typed function that decides per call; higher-risk flows can re-validate inputs and opt into hmac signing — a cryptographic stamp that ties the approval token to the exact tool inputs, so an attacker can't swap the arguments between the 'approve' click and the actual run. (2) durable workflows. the new workflowagent (from @ai-sdk/workflow ) persists execution state to durable storage between steps , so the agent survives a deploy, a process restart, or an interruption — and crucially, a human can approve hours later and the agent resumes from exactly that point instead of starting the task over. durable output streams mean a client can disconnect and reconnect and pick the stream back up. why it matters for us: 'ai that can act' is only safe if a human can veto the irreversible step and the system can wait patiently for that veto. v7 makes both a few lines of config instead of a custom state machine — the difference between a demo and something we'd point at a client's live account.","doit":"(1) upgrade: npm i ai@7 @ai-sdk/openai@7 (requires node 22+ and esm — add \"type\": \"module\" to package.json), then run the auto-migration npx @ai-sdk/codemod v7 (it also renames system → instructions and moves opentelemetry to @ai-sdk/otel ). (2) build the agent with toolloopagent from ai (think → call tool → read result → repeat). (3) gate the scary tools: set toolapproval to 'user-approval' for anything that spends money / sends / deletes; leave read-only tools on 'auto-approve' . (4) handle the pause in your ui: render the pending action + arguments, get the human's decision, resume the run; for high-stakes actions enable hmac signing . (5) make it durable: for long or human-gated jobs use workflowagent ( @ai-sdk/workflow ) so state survives restarts and delayed approvals. (6) on v6? npx skills add vercel/ai --skill migrate-ai-sdk-v6-to-v7 , then tell your coding agent 'use the migrate-ai-sdk-v6-to-v7 skill,' and review the diff against the official v7 migration guide. (7) cap the spend: pair it with a gateway hard cap (card-webdev-ai-gateway-spend-limits) so an approved-but-looping agent still can't blow the budget. card: card-webdev-ai-sdk-7-agents.","note":"corroboration: reported (2) — vercel primary (the ai sdk 7 changelog + the 'new programming model for durable execution' blog + the human-in-the-loop kb guide, all vercel.com) plus independent coverage (alphasignal's v7 write-up). honest scope: a major-version upgrade — node 22+ and esm are required, and the codemod handles syntax but not every semantic change, so read the migration guide before shipping; durable workflows need a configured durable store; tool-approval and hmac are opt-in (off by default), so an un-gated tool still runs without asking. advances the durable-execution thread (vercel workflow sdk → ai sdk's workflowagent) and the agent-safety-scaffold throughline (related: card-webdev-vercel-cancelable-jobs, card-ai-tooling-model-portability). logged a prediction (a 2nd major js agent framework ships built-in tool-approval + state-persisting durable execution). sources: vercel changelog (ai sdk 7), vercel blog (durable execution), vercel kb (human-in-the-loop agents), ai sdk migration guide."}]},{"h":"email — run it today: win back, then sunset, your disengaged subscribers — the list-hygiene move that protects inbox placement, not just your open rate","blocks":[{"sub":"litmus (jun 2026): mailbox providers grade you on whether people want your mail, so a big pool of never-openers teaches gmail/yahoo/outlook your sends are unwanted — and they route them to spam for your engaged buyers too. the fix is a standing flow: define disengaged by behaviour (no click/visit/purchase in 6+ months, not opens), run a 2–3 email re-engagement series, then suppress the non-responders and prune invalid/spam-trap addresses","tags":["email","deliverability","re-engagement","sunset-flow","list-hygiene","lifecycle"],"p":"what changed / the technique (a durable lifecycle play, resurfaced by a fresh litmus piece): the instinct when engagement sags is to a/b the subject line. the bigger lever is usually list size . mailbox providers decide inbox-vs-spam largely on engagement history — are recipients opening, clicking, replying, or are they ignoring and marking spam? send month after month to a large block of people who never engage and you teach gmail/yahoo/microsoft that your mail isn't wanted, which depresses placement for the active subscribers who actually buy. so the highest-roi email move this week isn't a new tool — it's pruning. the discipline: (a) identify disengaged subscribers by behaviour , not opens — apple mail privacy protection auto-opens emails, so open rate overstates interest; lean on click-to-open rate, site visits, and conversions , and a clear inactivity window (litmus's rule of thumb: no interaction in more than six months ). (b) win them back with a short re-engagement series before you cut them. (c) suppress the ones who still don't respond — stop sending, don't keep them on the file 'just in case.' (d) clean invalid/hard-bounced addresses, some of which are spam traps (recycled or pristine addresses that mailbox providers use to catch senders who don't practise list hygiene). why it matters for us: this is the same 'deliverability = wantedness' truth behind gmail's new postmaster verdict (card-email-postmaster-deliverability-analysis) and the inbox-placement audit (card-email-inbox-placement-audit) — and it's the cheapest of the three to act on. a smaller, engaged list reliably out-earns a big, ignored one because more of it reaches the inbox.","doit":"(1) build the segment: in your esp (klaviyo, omnisend, mailchimp, hubspot) define 'disengaged' = no click, site visit, or purchase in 6+ months (don't use opens as the sole signal). (2) run a re-engagement series (2–3 emails over ~2 weeks): a plain 'do you still want these?' with a one-click confirm, a best-of/value reminder, and a last-chance email with a real incentive if margins allow. (3) route the results: clickers/buyers → back into active flows; non-responders → suppress (stop mailing). (4) prune hard-bounced and invalid addresses so you're not feeding spam traps. (5) measure the lift: open gmail's free postmaster tools 'deliverability analysis' and note the verdict before, then ~3–4 weeks after pruning. (6) make it permanent: set the inactivity window as an automated sunset flow so people roll through win-back → suppression on their own, and run a seed/spam-placement test (card-email-inbox-placement-audit) to confirm the inbox-rate improvement. card: card-email-reengagement-sunset.","note":"corroboration: single-source primary (litmus, a recognized email-deliverability authority) for the specific guidance; the underlying mechanism (engagement history drives placement; spam-trap risk on stale lists) is well-established across deliverability practice and our own deliverability cards. honest scope: the 6-month window is a starting heuristic — high-consideration/low-frequency businesses (e.g. furniture, b2b) may warrant longer; suppression shrinks reach, so expect lower gross volume but higher inbox rate and revenue-per-send. advances the email deliverability thread (the engagement/list-hygiene leg, distinct from auth (card-email-dmarcbis-np), the postmaster verdict, and the ipr seed test). logged a prediction (a major esp ships an automated engagement-based suppression / 'sunset' default tied to a deliverability-risk signal). source: litmus."}]},{"h":"paid — run it today (google-ads): switch standard shopping to 'maximize conversion value' to bid for revenue while keeping the campaign you can actually see into","blocks":[{"sub":"june 2026: google ads is rolling out maximize conversion value bidding for standard shopping without a required target roas — so you can optimize for revenue without moving to a feed-only performance max campaign and losing search-term visibility/control; separately the old 'maximize conversion value with a target roas' strategy is being renamed simply 'target roas' (same behaviour)","tags":["paid","google-ads","shopping","smart-bidding","value-based-bidding"],"p":"what changed / the technique: google ads is extending maximize conversion value bidding to standard shopping campaigns, and — the part that matters — without requiring a target roas (target return on ad spend = revenue ÷ ad cost). the significance is structural. maximizing conversions tells google to get you the most orders at the lowest cost, which quietly favours cheap, low-value purchases; maximizing conversion value tells it to chase the most revenue , so a £200 basket is worth ten £20 ones. until now, getting value-based bidding generally pushed advertisers toward a feed-only performance max campaign — a more automated, more opaque type where you lose search-term reports and granular control. this change lets you keep standard shopping's transparency and steering and still bid for revenue. separately, as housekeeping, google is renaming the existing 'maximize conversion value with a target roas' strategy to just 'target roas' across the ui in june 2026 — the underlying behaviour is identical, so don't read the old name's disappearance as a feature being removed. why it matters for us: it removes a long-standing reason teams felt forced into pmax (value bidding), and it lets us run a fair head-to-head — same revenue goal, but in a campaign where we can see the queries and protect the brand/junk terms.","doit":"(1) confirm value tracking first: value bidding is blind without it — in tools → conversions verify your purchase conversion passes a real value (revenue), not just a count. (2) switch the strategy: open the standard shopping campaign → settings → bidding → change bid strategy → maximize conversion value . (3) leave the target roas box empty to start, so google spends the budget chasing maximum revenue with no efficiency floor while it learns. (4) give it room: hold budget steady for 2–3 weeks (smart bidding needs enough conversions to calibrate) and judge it on revenue and roas , not cost-per-click. (5) add a floor when ready: once the revenue level looks right, set a target roas near your recent achieved roas — not an aspirational number, which would choke delivery. (6) re-litigate pmax: if you only adopted feed-only pmax to unlock value bidding, test the same goal back in standard shopping as a guarded experiment (card-paid-aimax-dsa-experiment) and compare revenue, roas, and wasted-query rate. card: card-paid-standard-shopping-max-value.","note":"corroboration: reported (2) — search engine land + search engine roundtable (independent trade press), with google ads help documenting the maximize-conversion-value strategy itself. honest scope: staged rollout — it may not be on every account yet, and exact availability/labels can lag; the 'target roas' rename is cosmetic (behaviour unchanged) but will confuse anyone following an old sop, so update internal docs the same week (the recurring lesson: when a platform renames a campaign/bid type, fix onboarding steps immediately). treat 'value bidding beats conversion-count bidding' as account-specific — it only helps if your conversion values genuinely vary and are tracked accurately. advances the platform-ai-defaults thread on the control axis (a rare case of google giving control back , not collapsing it into a black box). logged a prediction (the 'target roas' rename completes platform-wide and/or maximize-conversion-value-without-troas extends beyond standard shopping). sources: search engine land, search engine roundtable, google ads help."}]},{"h":"news — the ai landscape (cross-checked; us markets closed for the weekend, so this is the what-companies-are-building half)","blocks":[{"sub":"with the frontier tier gated (mythos restricted to ~100 us orgs; gpt-5.6 customer-by-customer), the vacuum is filling with open/chinese models and a tightening compute crunch: z.ai's open-weight glm-5.2 reportedly nears top us models at finding security bugs (but the parity claim rests on a narrow semgrep test — read it skeptically); google capped meta's gemini use because it couldn't supply the capacity (bloomberg+ft); and wired reports an underground market reselling claude api access in china","tags":["news","ai-tooling","markets","policy"],"p":"what moved (all cross-checked; weekend, no live prices) — this advances yesterday's 'frontier is now gated' story to its consequence: the gap is being filled from below and the bottleneck is compute. (1) open weights close in — with an asterisk. z.ai's glm-5.2 (open-weight: the model file is downloadable and runs on your own hardware) drew jun 28 headlines for reportedly matching top us models at finding security bugs . read it with the cross-check the moment demands: the parity claim traces to a narrow semgrep benchmark (≈39% f1 on one vulnerability class — idor — vs ~32–37% for claude code), and practitioners including vercel's guillermo rauch pushed back that scaffolding and harness design , not the raw model, drive those numbers. so: directionally impressive, not verified parity with the restricted mythos. why it matters: a freely downloadable model that's close on real work is exactly the hedge for a gated frontier — it can't be switched off by anyone, which is why it belongs in your fallback list (card-ai-tooling-glm-5-2-route, card-ai-tooling-model-portability). (cross-check: venturebeat + interconnects + latent space on glm-5.2's coding strength; semgrep's own writeup + rauch on the security-parity caveat — treat the bug-finding figure as directional.) (2) compute scarcity bites a giant. google capped meta's use of gemini because it couldn't provide all the computing capacity meta wanted to buy (google told meta around march; the shortfall disrupted some meta ai projects, and meta has told staff to use ai tokens more efficiently). when the company selling the model has to ration its biggest customer, 'compute is the binding constraint' stops being a slogan. (confirmed ≥3: bloomberg + financial times (originating) + business standard.) (3) the gating has a black market. wired reports a thriving underground economy for claude access in china , with 'transfer station' sites buying api tokens abroad and reselling them to local users — a vivid sign that when capability is fenced off by geography/policy, demand routes around it. (single-source: wired via techmeme; treat specifics as reported.) why it matters for us — the climate signal, sharpened from yesterday: (a) the best model may not be in your account on day one , and the runner-up increasingly comes as open weights you host yourself — so keep at least one open-weight model (glm-class) in the fallback list and test it against your real tasks now, not in a crisis. (b) capacity, not just capability, is now a planning input — if google has to ration meta, smaller buyers should assume rate limits and price drift, and design features to degrade gracefully (card-webdev-ai-gateway-spend-limits, card-ai-tooling-model-portability). (c) trust the benchmark you ran, not the headline — the glm-5.2 security claim is the week's case study in why a 'matches the us frontier' headline needs the harness-and-scaffolding asterisk before it changes a tooling decision. open predictions check: markets were closed, so nothing resolved this weekend; the jun 8 'ai selloff is a dip, not a regime change' [news, due ~jul 20] stays open ; the jun 8 'power/compute is the binding constraint' [news, due ~aug 3] gains support from google rationing meta — open, leaning held ; the jun 19 'glm-5.2 reaches openrouter's coding top-3' [ai-tooling, due ~jul 19] gains momentum from this week's open-weight push. logged a new prediction (a 2nd hyperscaler publicly rations/caps a major customer's ai compute within ~8 weeks). sources: venturebeat (glm-5.2 coding benchmarks), interconnects (glm-5.2 analysis), bloomberg + financial times (google caps meta's gemini), business standard (capacity-constraint corroboration), techmeme (wired — underground claude market; wsj — glm-5.2 security claim)."}]}]

title: Daily Briefing — Saturday, June 27
q: daily briefing — saturday, june 27 saturday, june 27, 2026 web-design play of the day: stop re-explaining your design system to ai every time — package your team's rules into one figma 'skill' the design agent obeys on command. at config 2026 (jun 24 2026) figma's on-canvas design agent went to open beta for everyone and gained custom skills (plus mcp connectors and file/pdf context). a skill is a reusable instruction set you invoke as a slash command: name it follow-ds-guidelines and it becomes /follow-ds-guidelines . write your conventions once in plain markdown — which library/components to use, token names, the spacing scale and type ramp, do/don't rules, accessibility minimums — and every teammate's agent designs to your system instead of generic defaults. the #1 failure of ai-in-design is output that ignores your tokens and forces rework; a skill is the governance lever that fixes it, no engineering required. run-it-today: open the agent's prompt box → skills → add skill → start from scratch , paste your ds rules as numbered steps + one worked example, then test with /follow-ds-guidelines design a settings page . (open beta, free of ai credits during beta — credits apply at ga; needs a paid plan (pro/org/enterprise) with ai on and a full seat for design/make files; not on starter/education/gov. new agent threads are visible to full-seat teammates by default — set sensitive chats private. distinct from code layers , which is closed beta starting july.) growth play: google now shows you — first-party — how often your pages appear inside ai overviews and ai mode. open the report and set your baseline today. google search console has a generative ai performance report (a dedicated view of your impressions in google's ai features), and on jun 25 2026 john mueller publicly clarified exactly how those impressions are counted. until now ai-search visibility could only be estimated by third-party tools (semrush, ahrefs) — this is the source of truth, straight from google, segmentable by page/country/device. the play: open performance → generative ai , export your top and bottom urls by ai-feature impressions, date-stamp it as your baseline, then double down on the pages already winning ai citations and rewrite the gaps to match. read the numbers correctly per mueller: an impression fires only when a real link to your page is shown (a favicon alone doesn't count); links that require a user to expand them count only once expanded; the same site shown twice in one ai answer is one impression. (beta, rolling out to a subset of properties — you may not see it yet; impressions only right now, no clicks/ctr/queries. this resolves our jun 21 'google ships a first-party ai-visibility metric' call — ✅ with a caveat: the report had actually launched jun 3, before we logged the call, and it's impressions-only, not a bing-style citation- share metric.) ai-tooling play: hand a fast, cheap model the boring browser work that has no api — and let it click through it for you, in a sandbox. on jun 24 2026 google moved 'computer use' into mainline gemini 3.5 flash ( gemini-3.5-flash ) as a built-in tool: one model that sees a screen and acts — clicks, types, scrolls, navigates — across browser, mobile and desktop, in public preview via the gemini api. it scores 78.4 on osworld-verified (a hair behind gpt-5.5's 78.7) at $1.50 in / $9 out per million tokens (~⅓ of gpt-5.5), so it's cheap enough to run daily. it kills gui-bound grunt work with no export button: pulling numbers out of ad/analytics dashboards, filling repetitive intake/qa forms, scheduled competitor screenshots, smoke-testing your own app's ui before launch. run-it-today: try the zero-code browserbase demo linked from the docs, grab a key in google ai studio , then clone google's docker-sandboxed reference repo so the agent never touches your real machine. critical safety: agents that browse live pages are exposed to prompt injection (malicious instructions hidden in a page — attacks are already being reported) — before any real task, flip on the two opt-in safeguards (confirm-before-sensitive-actions + auto-halt-on-injection, both off by default), and layer a sandbox + website allowlist + human-in-the-loop. google says no single safeguard is enough. also moved — the ai landscape (cross-checked; markets closed, weekend): the frontier's best models are now shipping under explicit us-government gatekeeping. anthropic's claude mythos 5 is back — the us government notified anthropic it can be redeployed to a defined set of us critical-infrastructure operators (reported as 100+ companies and agencies; commerce secretary lutnick: 'appropriate safeguards are in place'). this resolves our jun 13 call ✅ (anthropic restores access to at least one of the two suspended models) — but it's a restricted re-release: fable 5 general access is still suspended , no timeline. meanwhile openai began a limited preview of gpt-5.6 in three variants ( sol flagship, terra everyday, luna fast/cheap) — and at the white house's request the government approves access customer-by-customer during preview (openai: 'we don't believe this kind of government access process should become the long-term default'). and the ai-memory crunch is now hitting consumer hardware: apple raised mac and ipad prices on jun 25, explicitly blaming surging memory/storage-chip costs from the ai data-center buildout ('we have never seen a component price increase this much, this quickly') — a demand-confirming signal for the same ai-memory trade (sk hynix/samsung/micron). (also: ibm debuted the first sub-1nm chip tech, ~5 years from production.) sharpen your edge: today's throughline is state your rules once, then make the machine read its own reality. a figma skill states your design system once so every agent obeys it; google's ai report makes you read your actual ai-search presence instead of guessing; gemini's computer-use agent reads the live screen and acts on it — inside a sandbox you control. and the landscape just drew the boundary on all of it: the very best models now arrive gated and approved, while the ai-memory bill lands on a laptop near you. plan client work around the tier you can actually get today, write your conventions down so the ai inherits them, and keep a human on the kill-switch. web-design growth ai-tooling news [{"h":"web design — run it today: teach the figma design agent your design system once, as a reusable skill (slash command) every teammate's agent obeys","blocks":[{"sub":"config 2026 (jun 24 2026): figma's on-canvas design agent went to open beta for everyone and gained custom skills, mcp connectors and file/pdf context — a skill is a markdown instruction set you invoke as a slash command (name it 'follow-ds-guidelines' → '/follow-ds-guidelines') so the agent designs to your tokens/components/rules instead of generic defaults; you can also upload a .md skill (agent skills spec) or attach a brief and connect github/slack/notion/linear","tags":["web-design","figma","design-systems","ai-agent","skills","governance"],"p":"what changed / the technique: at config 2026 on jun 24 2026 figma launched its on-canvas design agent to everyone (open beta) and gave it three ways to take on your context — custom skills , mcp connectors , and file/pdf/spreadsheet attachments. the one to run today is skills . a skill is a reusable, shareable instruction set — written in plain markdown — that you invoke as a slash command : the skill's name becomes the command, so a skill named follow-ds-guidelines is invoked as /follow-ds-guidelines . inside it you encode your team's actual conventions: which component library to pull from, your token names, the spacing scale and type ramp, do/don't rules, accessibility minimums (contrast, hit-target size). figma's how-to recommends an ordered instructions section plus at least one concrete examples block showing input → expected output. because the skill is just a file, you can also upload a .md written to the agent skills spec (the same format coding agents use) and reuse it across tools, and you can attach a brief or wire an mcp connector (github, slack, notion, atlassian, hex, linear) to pull live tickets/data into the design and write updates back. why it matters for us: the single most common failure of ai-in-design is output that ignores your design system — wrong components, off-scale spacing, invented tokens — which means rework that eats the time the ai was supposed to save. a skill turns your design-system rules into a portable, version-controllable instruction the agent obeys on command, so 'ai that guesses' becomes 'ai that follows our system.' it's a governance lever for design systems, available now, no engineering required.","doit":"(1) check access: you need a paid plan (professional/organization/enterprise) with ai features enabled and a full seat to use the agent in design/make files (view/dev/collab seats only get it in draft files); it's not on starter/education/government. (2) create the skill: open the agent's prompt box → skills → add skill → start from scratch ; fill name (becomes your slash command), description (when to use it), and instructions (markdown). (3) encode your ds in the instructions as numbered, imperative steps — the library/components to use, token names, spacing scale, type ramp, do/don't rules, a11y minimums — and add one examples block (a request → the correct output). click add . (4) test it: type /follow-ds-guidelines plus a request (e.g. 'design a settings page using our ds') and check the output respects your rules; iterate the instructions text until it does. (5) scale it: reuse an existing skill via skills → add skill → upload a file (drag in a .md per the agent skills spec). (6) add live context where useful: attach a brief/pdf to the thread, or add an mcp connector to pull a ticket's spec into the design. (7) privacy: as of jun 23 2026 new agent threads are visible by default to full-seat teammates with edit access — set sensitive chats private. card: card-webdesign-figma-agent-skills.","note":"corroboration: reported (2) — figma's own primary posts (the 'agent: custom tools, context, skills' blog, the config 2026 recap, and the help center 'custom skills for the figma agent and figma make' how-to — all figma.com) plus independent press (web developer's config 2026 write-up, which describes the agent's skills 'like agent skills in coding tools' + mcp connectors). honest scope: open beta as of jun 24 2026, rolling out gradually and free of ai credits during beta — credits will apply at ga (and prompting the agent to build plugins/shaders already costs credits), so cost it before standardizing a batch workflow; gated to paid plans + full seats for design/make (draft-only on view/dev/collab; excluded: starter/education/gov); custom skills are instructions-only (no scripts/ / references/ / assets/ dirs). distinct from code layers ('code on the canvas'), which is closed beta, early access starting july via figma.com/config-betas — not run-it-today — and from figma motion (card-webdesign-figma-motion) and shader fills/effects (card-graphic-figma-shaders), the other config 2026 drops. joins the design-tooling/handoff branch. logged a prediction (skills become billable ai-credit actions at ga and/or a community skills marketplace + a cross-tool .md skill standard emerge). sources: figma blog (agent skills), figma learn (custom skills how-to), figma blog (config 2026 recap), web developer."}]},{"h":"growth — run it today: open google's first-party ai-visibility report in search console, baseline your ai overviews / ai mode impressions, and read them correctly per mueller's counting rules","blocks":[{"sub":"google search console now has a generative ai performance report — your impressions inside ai overviews, ai mode and ai features in discover, segmentable by page/country/device — and on jun 25 2026 john mueller clarified how impressions are counted (a link to your page must actually be shown; a favicon doesn't count; expandable links count only once expanded; the same site twice in one answer = one impression). it's the first-party source of truth that replaces third-party aeo estimators","tags":["growth","aeo","ai-search","search-console","measurement","google"],"p":"what changed / the technique: google search console exposes a generative ai performance report — a dedicated view of how often your urls appear inside google's ai overviews , ai mode , and ai features in discover — and on jun 25 2026 google's john mueller publicly clarified the rules for how those impressions are counted. this is significant because, until now, ai-search visibility could only be inferred by third-party estimators (semrush's free checker, ahrefs brand radar, bing's ai citation share — all of which we've covered). this is the first-party source of truth from google , segmentable by page, country, device and date. the counting rules matter for reading it honestly (mueller, jun 25): an impression is logged only when an actual link to your page is shown in the ai feature — a favicon or icon with no link does not count; a link that requires the user to activate/expand it counts only once expanded (so the reported number can look lower than your true presence); and if the same site appears twice in one ai answer, that's one impression. separately, in the classic performance report the same url appearing in both an ai overview and the blue links is counted as one impression at the aio's position — so don't double-count across reports. why it matters for us: buyers increasingly start at an ai answer, and we've been optimizing aeo against estimates. getting an early, dated baseline from google's own data — while it's still in beta and competitors aren't watching — lets us correlate specific content changes to actual ai-feature visibility, instead of guessing.","doit":"(1) open the report: in google search console pick your property → performance in the left nav; if you're in the rollout, a generative ai report sits alongside search results and discover. (2) don't see it? it's gated to a subset of properties — verify the property (domain-level helps) and check the discover variant too; it may simply not be enabled for you yet. (3) read the pages breakdown: pull your top-3 and bottom-3 urls by ai-feature impressions — your 'already cited by google ai' winners and your gaps. (4) segment by country and device to see where your ai visibility concentrates; export to a sheet and date-stamp it as your baseline . (5) act on it: map the high-impression urls against the aeo formats that win citations (intent-matched title, one-sentence answer up top, list/table, an original first-party stat, faq schema + last-updated date — see card-growth-aeo-content-formats and card-growth-schema-aeo); double down on those patterns and rewrite low-impression pages to match. (6) track over time: there's no api field yet, so set a recurring weekly export so you can prove cause-and-effect after you ship changes. (7) interpret with the counting rules above so you don't over- or under-read a number. card: card-growth-ai-visibility-check (updated).","note":"corroboration: reported (2) — google's own primary docs (search console help 'generative ai performance report' for search and for discover, plus the search central blog announcement — all google.com) + search engine journal (mueller's impression-counting explanation, jun 25, independent). honest scope: beta, rolling out to a subset of website owners — you may not have it yet, and early access has looked uneven by region; it is impressions only right now (no clicks, ctr, average position, or query data in this report — verified in google's help doc; one auto-summary that claimed clicks/ctr was a misread); and it groups ai overviews + ai mode under 'generative ai features,' so you can't cleanly isolate one from the other. resolves the open jun 21 prediction ('google ships a first-party ai-citation/answer-visibility metric in search console') — ✅ on the substance, with an honest caveat: the report had already launched jun 3 2026 (before we logged the jun 21 call — we missed it; the jun 25 mueller clarification is what resurfaced it), and it's impressions-only , not the competitor citation- share metric the call benchmarked against bing. advances the answer-engine-optimization thread (first-party measurement leg). logged a new prediction (google adds clicks and/or ctr — possibly query data — to the generative ai performance report before ga). sources: google search console help (search), google search console help (discover), google search central blog, search engine journal."}]},{"h":"ai tooling — run it today: point gemini 3.5 flash 'computer use' at your repetitive browser ops and let it click through them, inside a docker sandbox you control","blocks":[{"sub":"jun 24 2026: google moved 'computer use' into mainline gemini 3.5 flash (gemini-3.5-flash) as a built-in tool — one fast/cheap model that sees a screen and acts (click, type, scroll, navigate, drag) across browser/mobile/desktop, in public preview via the gemini api; 78.4 on osworld-verified (vs gpt-5.5's 78.7) at $1.50 in / $9 out per m tokens (~⅓ of gpt-5.5), with a zero-code browserbase demo and an official docker-sandboxed reference repo","tags":["ai-tooling","gemini","computer-use","agents","automation","browser","prompt-injection"],"p":"what changed / the technique: on jun 24 2026 google moved its 'computer use' capability from a standalone model into mainline gemini 3.5 flash ( gemini-3.5-flash ) as a native, built-in tool. one fast, cheap model can now see a screen, reason, and act — clicking, typing, scrolling, navigating and dragging — across browser, mobile and desktop environments, available in public preview today via the gemini api (and the gemini enterprise agent platform, the renamed vertex ai). it scores 78.4 on osworld-verified (the standard computer-use benchmark) — within a fraction of gpt-5.5's 78.7 — at $1.50 in / $9 out per million tokens (~one-third of gpt-5.5's $5/$30), so it's cheap and fast enough to run on a schedule. the action loop is simple: the model returns a function call (an action + its reasoning + normalized 0–999 coordinates); your client scales the coordinates to the viewport, executes the action (google's reference uses playwright ), captures a fresh screenshot, and sends it back — repeating until the task is done. why it matters for us: this kills the long tail of gui-bound grunt work that has no api and no export button — pulling numbers out of ad/analytics dashboards that won't export, filling repetitive intake/qa forms, scheduled competitor screenshots, bulk updates in a content portal, and smoke-testing your own web app's ui flows before a launch. at flash price/speed those become a daily loop, not a manual chore.","doit":"(1) feel the loop with zero code: open the browserbase-hosted demo linked from the official docs and give it a plain-english task ('open this dashboard, read the 7-day spend, screenshot it'). (2) get an api key in google ai studio (ai.google.dev), prototype a prompt, then hit 'get code' to export the gemini api call in your language. (3) clone the official reference repo ( google-gemini/computer-use-preview ) — it ships a ready-to-use docker sandbox so the agent never touches your real machine. (4) set the model to gemini-3.5-flash with the computer-use tool enabled (you can co-activate search, code execution and function calling on the same agent). (5) flip on the two opt-in safeguards before any real task — they're off by default: (a) require explicit user confirmation on sensitive/irreversible actions, (b) auto-terminate the task on detected prompt injection. (6) lock the blast radius: run only in the docker/vm sandbox, apply a website allowlist , keep a human in the loop for purchases/sends, and turn on audit logging. (7) start low-stakes: point it at an internal task first (e.g. a ui regression pass on your staging site) before anything that spends money or sends messages. card: card-ai-tooling-gemini-computer-use.","note":"corroboration: confirmed (3) — google primary (the blog.google announcement + the ai.google.dev gemini api 'computer use' docs; note the deepmind blog url 302-redirects to the same google post, so it's not an independent source) + the next web + search engine journal (the security angle). honest scope: public preview — google's own docs warn it 'may contain security vulnerabilities' and to supervise closely; access is via the gemini api / enterprise agent platform, pay-as-you-go, no special gating beyond a key (genuinely run-it-today, unlike gpt-5.6 'sol' which stays us-gov / customer-by-customer gated — see today's news). the big caveat is indirect prompt injection : agents that browse live pages can be hijacked by malicious instructions hidden in a page or document, and press is already reporting real attacks (e.g. a poisoned skills file driving unauthorized purchases) — google explicitly says 'no individual safeguard is sufficient,' so layer sandbox + allowlist + human confirmation + injection auto-halt + logging. prices/benchmarks directional. joins the agent-autonomy/safety-scaffold thread (related: card-ai-tooling-claude-loop-goal, card-ai-tooling-model-portability). logged a prediction (a 2nd frontier lab ships a fast-tier computer-use/browser-control model at ≤~$2/$10 per m tokens to match gemini 3.5 flash). sources: google (blog.google), google ai for developers (gemini api docs), the next web, search engine journal."}]},{"h":"news — the ai landscape (cross-checked; us markets closed for the weekend, so this is the what-companies-are-building half)","blocks":[{"sub":"the frontier's best models now ship under explicit us-government gatekeeping: anthropic's claude mythos 5 is back but restricted to ~100+ us critical-infrastructure operators (fable 5 general access still suspended, no timeline) — resolving our jun 13 restoration call ✅ — while openai's gpt-5.6 (sol/terra/luna) ships via a customer-by-customer government-approved preview; separately apple raised mac/ipad prices jun 25, blaming ai-driven memory-chip cost spikes (a demand-confirming signal for the sk hynix/samsung/micron memory trade)","tags":["news","ai-tooling","markets","policy"],"p":"what moved (all cross-checked; weekend, no live prices): the throughline this week is frontier-model availability becoming a government decision . (1) anthropic's claude mythos 5 is restored — but restricted. the us government notified anthropic that mythos 5 (its cybersecurity model, dark since the jun 12 export-control directive) can be redeployed to a defined set of us critical-infrastructure operators — reported as 100+ companies and agencies — under agreed safeguards (commerce secretary lutnick: 'appropriate safeguards are in place'). anthropic's statement: it has been 'working closely with the us government to restore access' and is still working toward the return of claude fable 5 for unrestricted use — which remains suspended with no timeline . this resolves our jun 13 prediction ✅ ('anthropic restores access to at least one of the two models') — honestly, a partial/restricted restoration, not a full one. (confirmed ≥3: engadget, nbc news, cnn headlines + anthropic's own statement via cybersecurity news; the exact recipient count varies by outlet — treat '~100+' as directional, and don't conflate it with a separate jun 2 glasswing-expansion '150 orgs/15 countries' figure.) (2) openai's gpt-5.6 ships gated. openai began a limited preview of gpt-5.6 in three variants — sol (flagship/strongest), terra (balanced everyday), luna (fast/cheap) — to a small group of trusted partners, and at the white house's request the government approves access customer-by-customer during preview (under the office of the national cyber director + ostp), with broad release 'in the coming weeks.' openai: 'we don't believe this kind of government access process should become the long-term default.' (confirmed ≥3: openai's own wording via simon willison, engadget, the decoder; the specific partner count (~20) is single-source/directional. variant pricing per openai, directional: sol $5/$30, terra $2.50/$15, luna $1/$6 per m tokens.) (3) the ai-memory crunch reaches consumer hardware. apple raised prices on macs and ipads on jun 25 (iphone/watch/airpods spared), explicitly blaming surging memory/storage-chip costs as makers prioritize ai data-center orders — 'we have never seen a component price increase this much, this quickly' (e.g. macbook air 512gb $1,099→$1,299; ipad air 128gb $599→$749). it's the same shortage lifting sk hynix/samsung/micron — a demand-confirming, not demand-collapsing, signal for the ai-memory trade. (confirmed ≥3: fox business, macrumors + reuters/bloomberg headlines.) (4) quick hit: ibm debuted the first sub-1nm (0.7nm/7-angstrom) chip tech using 3d 'nanostack' transistors (~100b transistors on a fingernail; up to 50% more performance or 70% better efficiency vs 2nm) — but it's ~5 years from production , so a roadmap signal, not a near-term lever (confirmed ≥3: ibm newsroom, howtogeek + mit tech review/ars headlines). why it matters for us — the climate signal: the 'newest model, instantly available to everyone' era is over for the frontier tier. (a) the single best model may not be in your account on day one — scope client work around the generally-available tier (terra/luna-, sonnet-class), not an embargoed flagship. (b) 'which model, what governance, is it cleared?' is becoming a procurement question , not just a quality one — ai provenance is moving up the buying checklist. (c) budget for drift: apple's hikes show ai-memory demand now bleeds into hardware costs, so compute/device line items will creep up. thread impact: the frontier-model-governance thread advances from 'switched off overnight' (jun 12–13) to 'switched back on, but only for the cleared' — and the craft payoff is the model-portability scaffold (card-ai-tooling-model-portability). on the open v-shape 'dip not regime change' call [news, due ~jul 20]: markets were closed; nothing material broke this weekend, and apple's memory-cost hike reads as demand-confirming for the memory basket — call stays open, leaning held . logged two new predictions (fable 5 unrestricted access restored by ~sep 30; gpt-5.6 reaches ga without per-customer approval by ~aug 15). sources: engadget (mythos), cybersecurity news (anthropic statement), simon willison (openai gpt-5.6), engadget (gpt-5.6 variants), the decoder (customer-by-customer), fox business + macrumors (apple), ibm newsroom + howtogeek (ibm)."}]}]

title: Daily Briefing — Friday, June 26
q: daily briefing — friday, june 26 friday, june 26, 2026 web-dev play of the day: make your next.js app feel spa-instant on navigation — without rewriting to a spa — by flipping two flags. on jun 25 2026 next.js shipped 16.3 (preview) with instant navigations . turn on cachecomponents: true and every route's awaited data now gives you a choice: stream it (wrap in <suspense> → user sees an instant loading shell), cache it ( 'use cache' → user sees instant cached ui), or block it ( export const instant = false ). add partialprefetching: true and next prefetches one reusable shell per route instead of one request per link — killing the embarrassing prefetch-flurry in the network tab. bonus dev tooling: instant insights turns slow navigations into a dev-time error, an instant() playwright helper guards against regressions, and a navigation inspector shows exactly what each shell prefetches. run-it-today: on a branch, npm i next@preview , set both flags, open instant insights, and fix the one flagged route by wrapping its data in <suspense> or 'use cache' . (pre-production preview; flags are opt-in but slated to become defaults; use chrome/firefox for the dev tooling. same-day: ai sdk 7 also landed — agents, tool approvals, durable workflows, realtime voice/video.) graphic play (no photoshop round-trip): describe a texture in plain words and let figma build it as a live, tweakable material right on the canvas. at config 2026 (jun 24) figma shipped shader fills and effects — real webgpu shaders you make by prompting the figma agent or dropping a reference image. a fill is a new material; an effect transforms what's already in the layer. named looks include frosted glass, chrome, dithering, pixelation, halftone, particles , and stackable ones like gradient map, riso print, luminance particles . because each shader is parameterized by default , you get sliders on the canvas — so a brand treatment becomes a reusable, tweakable component, not a baked png. the play: stop hunting stock grain/glass textures and type the treatment you want, then dial it to brand. (open beta, rolling out jun 24 to full seats on a paid plan ; not on starter/education/gov; free of ai credits for now, charged at ga; interactive shaders 'coming soon'. distinct from yesterday's figma motion animation timeline and from code layers, which lands in july.) cro play (answers a teammate's ask-anything question — 'the best one thing for cro and aov?'): the honest answer is 'whatever your numbers say is leaking most' — and you can decide it in an hour with ai on your own export. export your last-90-day orders + ga4 funnel, hand the ai the order-value distribution, and ask it to compute your aov and your modal (most common) order value , then name the single highest-dollar leak. if it's basket size → a free-shipping threshold set just above your modal order value plus a 'you're $x from free shipping' progress bar is the single highest-leverage, no-dev aov lever (the 'goal-gradient' effect — people push harder as the reward nears). if it's checkout drop-off → kill friction first (guest checkout, fewer fields, show full cost incl. shipping early). then ship one change and measure aov, conversion and margin for 3–4 weeks. the point: stop copying someone else's playbook — let your data pick the lever. also moved — the ai landscape (cross-checked; market figures directional): the ai selloff has a new trigger today — not capex, not talent, but ipo timing. a report (nyt, via bloomberg) that openai leans toward delaying its ipo to 2027 — with sam altman holding firm on a target valuation of up to ~$1 trillion rather than listing sooner at a lower one, amid tech-stock volatility and spacex's rocky post-ipo trade — hammered ai names jun 26 . softbank (openai's biggest backer) fell as much as ~12–13% ; japan's nikkei −4.15% , korea's kospi −5.81% (circuit breaker tripped again), kioxia −12% . separately (the information): openai will ship gpt-5.6 only via a limited, customer-by-customer-approved preview at the white house's request. counter-signal the same week: sk hynix priced a record ~$29b nasdaq adr listing at ~$166/adr (255,500 won), eyeing a jul 10 debut (hsbc sees ~+20% upside) — money still chasing the ai-memory trade. this pressures our 'dip not regime change' v-shape call again (due ~jul 20): softbank is the basket's laggard and just dropped double-digits, while samsung/sk hynix sit well above their jun 8 mark — still open, leaning held but volatile. sharpen your edge: today's throughline is stop translating and start declaring — then let the system do the work . next.js lets you declare a navigation instant (stream/cache/block) instead of rebuilding as a spa; figma lets you describe a material instead of hand-painting it in photoshop; the cro play lets your data declare which lever leaks most instead of you guessing. and the market just drew the same line it's been drawing all month — it has stopped paying for ai story (an eventual $1t ipo) and wants the delivery (when, and at what return). decide from the real signal in front of you, ship one thing, measure it. web-dev graphic cro news [{"h":"web dev — run it today: make next.js navigations feel spa-instant with instant navigations (two flags, no spa rewrite)","blocks":[{"sub":"next.js 16.3 (preview, jun 25 2026) ships instant navigations: enable cachecomponents and each awaited route data becomes a choice — stream with (instant loading shell), cache with 'use cache' (instant cached ui), or block with export const instant = false — plus partial prefetching (partialprefetching) that prefetches one reusable shell per route instead of one request per link, with instant insights, an instant() playwright helper and a navigation inspector to keep routes honest","tags":["web-dev","nextjs","performance","prefetch","react","cache-components"],"p":"what changed: on jun 25 2026 the next.js team published 16.3 as a public preview, headlined by instant navigations — opt-in behaviors that bring single-page-app responsiveness to the server-driven app router without giving up server rendering. the mechanism: enable cachecomponents: true in next.config.ts , and wherever a route await s data on the server you now choose how the navigation feels — stream it by wrapping in <suspense> (the user instantly sees a loading shell, real ui streams in), cache it with 'use cache' (the user instantly sees previously cached ui), or deliberately block with export const instant = false for routes (a blog post, say) where you'd rather wait than show a shell. separately, partialprefetching: true rethinks prefetch: instead of firing a request per link in the viewport (the notorious network-tab flurry), next now prefetches one reusable shell per route and caches it for the session; opt specific links into deeper prefetch with <link prefetch> + 'use cache' , or extend to request-time content with export const prefetch = 'allow-runtime' . to keep it from regressing, instant insights makes a slow navigation an error in development , an instant() helper from @next/playwright asserts what must be visible without waiting for the network, and a navigation inspector in devtools pauses each navigation at its shell so you can see exactly what's prefetched. why it matters for us: 'navigation feels slow' is the single most common, most valid criticism of the server-components sites we ship for clients — and the usual fix is a painful rewrite toward a client spa. this closes that gap with config + a few <suspense> boundaries, and the per-route shell prefetch also trims wasted bandwidth on link-heavy pages (sidebars, chat lists, dashboards).","doit":"(1) on a branch , install the preview: npm install next@preview (this is pre-production — don't ship it to real users yet). (2) turn it on: in next.config.ts set cachecomponents: true and partialprefetching: true . (3) run dev and read instant insights — it flags routes whose navigation isn't instant (a dev-time error), so you get a worklist, not a guess. (4) fix the flagged route: wrap its slow awaited data in <suspense> (stream) or mark the function 'use cache' (cache); if a route genuinely should wait, opt out with export const instant = false . (5) inspect the shell: open the navigation inspector in devtools, pause at the shell, confirm the instant part is what you expect; add <link prefetch> + 'use cache' only where you want more than the shell to pop in. (6) lock it in: add an instant() playwright test ( import {'{ instant }'} from '@next/playwright' ) so a future refactor can't silently make the route slow again. (7) use the official cache components adoption skill (linked from the post) to have your coding agent walk an existing app through adoption. use chrome or firefox for the dev tooling — safari has known preview issues. card: card-webdev-nextjs-instant-navigations.","note":"grounding: primary, single-vendor — the next.js blog ('next.js 16.3: instant navigations', andrew clark / josh story, jun 25 2026) plus the preview instant navigations docs and the github discussion thread; vendor-confirmed feature, not a multi-source claim, so no corroboration count. honest scope: this is a pre-production preview published under the @preview npm tag — next says a 'polished stable release' is coming 'in the coming weeks' and that changes are likely before stable, so prototype on it, don't deploy to users; the behaviors are gated behind opt-in flags ( cachecomponents , partialprefetching ) that next plans to make defaults in a future major; known preview bugs include a params-in-shell blocking case that isn't reported by instant insights and broken instant insights tooling in safari (use chrome/firefox in dev); actual prefetching only runs in production. joins the next/vercel platform thread. logged a prediction (next.js 16.3 reaches stable ga with instant navigations and/or cachecomponents becomes a default). scan-line (not today's card): ai sdk 7 shipped the same day (jun 25) — agents with tool approvals, durable workflows, sandboxes, observability, and realtime voice/video generation; pnpm add ai@latest + an npx @ai-sdk/codemod v7 migration — worth a look if you build ai features. sources: next.js blog, next.js preview docs, vercel blog (ai sdk 7)."}]},{"h":"graphic — run it today: prompt figma to build a custom webgpu shader (frosted glass, chrome, halftone, grain) as a live, tweakable material — no photoshop round-trip","blocks":[{"sub":"figma shipped shader fills and effects at config 2026 (jun 24 2026): real webgpu shaders you create by describing the look to the figma agent or dropping a reference image — a 'fill' is a new material, an 'effect' transforms what's already in the layer; named looks include frosted glass, chrome, dithering, pixelation, halftone and particles, with stackable effects like gradient map, riso print and luminance particles, all parameterized by default so you get adjustable controls on the canvas","tags":["graphic","figma","shaders","webgpu","texture","brand"],"p":"what changed / the technique: at config 2026 on jun 24 2026 figma made shader creation a first-class, no-code part of the canvas with shader fills and effects (webgpu). instead of writing glsl or round-tripping to photoshop/after effects for a texture, you describe the look in plain words to the figma agent (or hand it a reference image ) and it builds the shader for you. the distinction: a shader fill acts as a new material on the layer, while a shader effect transforms what's already there . figma names looks like frosted glass, chrome, dithering, pixelation, halftone and particles , and shows stackable effects such as gradient map, riso print and luminance particles . crucially, every generated shader is parameterized by default — figma exposes the knobs as native-feeling controls directly on the canvas , so the result isn't a frozen image but a tweakable, shareable treatment. why it matters for us: custom surface treatments — glassmorphism panels, duotone/halftone hero art, film grain, chrome/metal type, dithered backgrounds — show up constantly in brand and ui work, and today they mean either buying stock textures or a photoshop detour that bakes the effect into pixels nobody can adjust later. prompt-to-shader keeps the treatment live and on-brand : a designer types the look, dials the on-canvas sliders to the brand palette, and the team can re-skin it without rebuilding. it turns 'find a grain texture' into 'describe the grain i want.'","doit":"(1) check access: you need a full seat on a paid figma plan (pro/org/enterprise) — it's an open beta rolling out from jun 24 2026, not available on starter/education/gov. (2) pick the right verb: select a shape/frame and add a shader fill when you want a new material (a glass surface, a gradient-mapped backdrop), or a shader effect when you want to transform existing art (halftone a photo, dither an illustration). (3) prompt the figma agent: describe the look — e.g. 'frosted-glass card surface with subtle noise' , 'halftone duotone in our brand red and ink' , 'brushed-chrome metal for this headline' — or drop a reference image for it to match. (4) dial it to brand: use the on-canvas controls figma exposes (it parameterizes the shader automatically) to set palette, scale, intensity; stack effects (e.g. gradient map + luminance particles) for a layered treatment. (5) make it reusable: keep the parameters tidy so teammates can re-skin the same shader for variants instead of regenerating from scratch. (6) sanity-check delivery: webgpu renders in-app — when you export to flat assets or hand to dev, confirm the look survives at the sizes/formats you ship. card: card-graphic-figma-shaders.","note":"corroboration: reported (2) — figma's own config 2026 recap blog + figma learn 'what's new from config 2026' help article (primary/vendor, same domain) and techtimes (independent press on the config 2026 shader/code-layers news). honest scope: open beta , gradually rolling out from jun 24 2026 and limited to full seats on paid plans (excluded: starter, education, government); shaders do not consume ai credits today but will at general availability , so factor cost in before standardizing a batch workflow; interactive shaders are 'coming soon' (not shipped); and this is distinct from two other config 2026 items — figma motion (yesterday's on-canvas animation timeline, card-webdesign-figma-motion) and code layers (early access in july, not run-it-today). webgpu support varies by browser/gpu — verify exported/handed-off fidelity. advances the design-tooling thread (related: card-webdesign-figma-motion, card-graphic-recraft-svg-vector, card-graphic-figma-capture-layers). logged a prediction (a 2nd major design tool ships prompt-to-gpu-shader generation, or figma's shaders exit open beta to ga). sources: figma blog (config 2026 recap), figma learn (what's new from config 2026), techtimes."}]},{"h":"cro & aov — run it today: stop guessing the 'one thing' — let ai read your own order + funnel data and name the single highest-dollar lever (answers a team ask-anything gap)","blocks":[{"sub":"a teammate asked the ask box 'what is the best one thing to focus on for cro and aov?' — the honest answer is it depends on your funnel, and you can decide it in under an hour: export last-90-day orders + ga4 funnel, have claude/chatgpt compute your aov and modal (most common) order value and name the biggest leak, then ship one lever — a modal-pegged free-shipping threshold + progress bar if it's basket size, or checkout-friction removal if it's drop-off","tags":["cro","aov","analytics","ai-analysis","ecommerce"],"p":"what this answers: this is a direct reply to an open ask-anything question in the team's demand backlog — 'the best one thing to focus on for cro and aov.' the trap is treating it as a universal answer; the leverage is decidable from your own numbers. there is no single 'best thing' that's true for every store — a store with a healthy aov but leaky checkout needs the opposite fix from one with a tidy checkout and tiny baskets. the run-it-today move is to let ai read your real distribution and tell you which lever leaks the most money, then pull exactly one. the default answers, if you must pick blind: for aov , the single highest-leverage, no-dev lever is a free-shipping threshold set just above your most common (modal) order value , paired with a live 'you're $x from free shipping' progress bar — it works via the 'goal-gradient' effect (shoppers push harder as a reward gets closer) and moves the whole basket distribution without buying any new traffic. for conversion , the single highest-leverage lever is removing checkout friction — guest checkout, fewer form fields, and showing the full price (including shipping) early, since surprise costs are the #1 documented reason carts get abandoned (baymard). why it matters for us: teams burn weeks debating cro tactics copied from blog posts; an hour with the store's own export replaces that debate with a ranked, dollar-weighted worklist — and tying the threshold to the modal order value (not a round number, and not the blunt 'set it ~30% above aov' rule of thumb) is what makes free shipping feel attainable to the most shoppers.","doit":"(1) pull the data: in shopify admin → orders → export (or your platform's order export) grab the last 90 days; from ga4 note where the funnel drops (product → cart → checkout → purchase). (2) ask the ai to diagnose: paste the order-value summary into claude/chatgpt (use its data-analysis mode for the raw csv) and prompt: 'from this order-value distribution, compute my aov and my modal (most common) order value, show the distribution shape, and tell me whether i lose more money to small baskets or to checkout drop-off given this funnel.' (3) if the leak is basket size (aov): set a free-shipping threshold just above your modal order value and add a cart/mini-cart progress bar ('you're $x from free shipping') with one relevant low-cost add-on beside it — the bar is the lift (see card-cro-free-shipping-threshold for the margin math). (4) if the leak is checkout drop-off: fix friction first — enable guest checkout (card-cro-guest-checkout), cut form fields (card-cro-minimise-form-fields), and show the full cost incl. shipping early (card-cro-show-total-cost-early). (5) bundle to the goal: ask the ai to mine co-purchase pairs from your export and propose 2–3 bundles priced to nudge orders over the threshold (card-cro-cross-sell-bundles). (6) ship one change and measure aov, conversion and margin for 3–4 weeks before the next — a higher aov that you fund with absorbed shipping can be a net loss. card: card-cro-ai-diagnose-one-lever.","note":"grounding: technique built on primary/authoritative sources — shopify's aov guide (the aov = revenue ÷ orders formula, the free-shipping-threshold guidance, and the caution to peg the threshold to the modal order value so it feels attainable) and baymard institute (checkout/cart-abandonment research: surprise/extra cost is the top abandonment reason). honest scope: the percentage rules of thumb (e.g. 'threshold ~30% above aov', upsell/bundle lifts in the ~15–25% range) are well-supported starting points to test, not guarantees — the whole point of the ai-diagnosis step is to replace folklore with your own distribution; and the ai's read is only as good as the export you give it, so sanity-check its aov/modal numbers against your platform's own analytics. covers the open cro/aov demand gap from the team's ask-anything backlog (gaps-digest). related: card-cro-free-shipping-threshold, card-cro-cross-sell-bundles, card-cro-post-purchase-upsell, card-cro-guest-checkout, card-cro-show-total-cost-early. logged a prediction (a major commerce platform or top aov app ships an ai threshold-advisor that recommends the number from a store's own order distribution). sources: shopify — average order value guide, baymard institute — cart abandonment."}]},{"h":"news — the ai landscape (cross-checked; all market figures directional, intraday vs close vary by source)","blocks":[{"sub":"the ai selloff gets a new trigger — ipo timing, not capex or talent: a report (nyt, via bloomberg) that openai leans toward delaying its ipo to 2027 (altman holding firm on a ~$1t valuation rather than listing sooner/lower, amid tech-stock volatility + spacex's rocky debut) slammed ai names jun 26 — softbank ~−12–13%, nikkei −4.15%, kospi −5.81% (circuit breaker again), kioxia −12% — while sk hynix priced a record ~$29b nasdaq adr at ~$166 (jul 10 target), money still chasing ai memory; this pressures our 'dip not regime change' v-shape call again (due ~jul 20)","tags":["news","markets","ai-tooling"],"p":"what moved: the month-long ai-equity repricing took a new shape on jun 26 — this time the trigger isn't capex fear (jun 22–23) or talent flight, it's ipo timing and valuation discipline . a report (originating with the new york times , carried by bloomberg) said openai is leaning toward delaying its ipo to 2027 : its bankers laid out two paths — list sooner at a lower number, or wait and push for a valuation of up to ~$1 trillion (up from a ~$730b last private mark) — and ceo sam altman is holding firm on the higher target , with advisers warning that tech-stock volatility and spacex's rocky post-ipo trade would dampen retail enthusiasm now. markets read a delayed ipo as delayed returns for openai's backers: softbank (its largest investor) fell as much as ~12–13% ; japan's nikkei 225 −4.15% , korea's kospi −5.81% (tripping a circuit breaker again), and japanese nand maker kioxia −12% as ai-memory names sold off. separately, per the information , altman told staff openai will release gpt-5.6 only through a limited preview approved customer-by-customer at the white house's request (the office of the national cyber director / ostp) — dovetailing with the jun 25 'white house asks openai to slow-roll its new model' thread. the counter-signal the same week: sk hynix priced a record ~$29b nasdaq adr offering at ~$166 per adr (255,500 won), targeting a jul 10 debut, with hsbc seeing ~+20% upside — i.e. capital is still actively chasing the ai-memory trade even as sentiment whipsaws. why it matters for us: this is a climate signal , not a trade — the market just split 'is ai real?' from 'when does it pay off, and at what return?' clients will increasingly ask about time-to-roi and whether the ai vendors you build on are durable through a delayed-liquidity stretch. lead pitches with near-term, measurable payoffs you can ship now, not a vendor's eventual blockbuster ipo. thread impact: a fresh leg of the ai-trade-repricing storyline that pressures our open 'dip not regime change' v-shape call [news, due ~jul 20]: softbank is the basket's laggard and just dropped double-digits again, though samsung and sk hynix sit well above their jun 8 reference (sk hynix near a record on the adr plan) — so the basket as a whole is likely still above jun 8 and the call stays open, leaning held but volatile ; the apple call [due ~jul 21] remains open. corroboration: confirmed (≥3) — bloomberg, yahoo finance, tradingkey, cnbc (+ nyt as the originating report; the information on gpt-5.6). all percentages directional, intraday vs close vary by source. sources: bloomberg, yahoo finance, tradingkey, cnbc."}]}]

title: Daily Briefing — Thursday, June 25
q: daily briefing — thursday, june 25 thursday, june 25, 2026 web-design play of the day: animate a design on the figma canvas — then copy the real css/react easing straight out of dev mode, no after effects round-trip. at config 2026 (jun 24) figma shipped figma motion : a real animation timeline as a fourth canvas mode next to design / draw / dev. you keyframe position, scale, rotation, opacity , drop fade/move/scale presets, define custom easing as reusable motion variables , scrub to preview, and export mp4/webm/gif/svg. the handoff win: in dev mode → motion a dev copies the exact timing + easing curve as css, json, or framework-ready react (motion.dev) — deleting the 'what easing did the designer mean?' guesswork. run-it-today: switch one frame to motion, keyframe a single button-hover or modal entrance, copy the css. (open beta, all plans; starter exports are limited; validate the exported curve against your front-end. code layers — code on the canvas — is separate and only rolls out in july.) social play (organic, free): stop guessing why a reel popped — ask the model that has your numbers. meta's creator assistant — a free conversational ai built into the facebook creator dashboard (live since jun 4 ; meta doubled down jun 24 with a standalone creator studio app built on it) — answers questions against your own account data and brainstorms ideas off live facebook trends . the play: ask 'why did my top reel outperform?' , pull out the repeatable variable (hook/length/audio), then ask 'give me 3 angles off what's trending in my niche' and turn that into the week's posts. it beats generic best-time charts and a blank chatgpt prompt because it sees your real performance. (geo-gated: us/canada/india for now; facebook dashboard only.) paid play [meta-ads]: stop building one ad per format — pack one ad with up to 10 mixed image + video assets across aspect ratios and let advantage+ pick the winner per placement. meta's refreshed multimedia-ads guidance (reported jun 24 , from meta's own help doc) is explicit: upload all your assets into a single ad and let delivery optimize — up to 10 images and videos , including multiple aspect ratios (9:16 + 1:1/4:5), in one ad. splitting creative per placement just splinters the learning signal. the operator edge moves back to creative volume + variety : five genuinely different angles (ugc clip, demo, testimonial, text-led explainer, lifestyle) beat fifty near-duplicates. keep advantage+ creative on; disclose any ai-generated asset. (per-ad cap is 10; that's distinct from total ad-set asset volume. figure traces to meta's doc as quoted by social media today — reported, not yet a 3-source claim.) also moved — the ai landscape (cross-checked; market figures directional): this is an 'ai capex is paying off' rebound week that flips the jun 22–23 fear. micron blew out fiscal q3 — revenue ~$41.5b (vs ~$35.7b est) and record 84.9% gross margin, guiding q4 to ~$50b ;
