hbm (high-bandwidth ai memory) is sold out through 2027 — stock jumped ~+16% premarket jun 25. openai + broadcom unveiled 'jalapeño' (jun 24), openai's first custom inference chip (tsmc 3nm, 8 hbm stacks, deploys end-2026). qualcomm is buying ai-compiler company modular (~$3.9b, all-stock) to chip at nvidia's cuda moat. and morgan stanley (jun 24) framed the selloff as a crowded dip, not broken roi — which resolves our jun 23 prediction ✅ (an ai-megacap/sell-side defends capex roi within ~2 weeks). counter-notes: cerebras sank on a soft margin guide; oracle shed ~21,000 roles over the year (its 10-k ties cuts to ai); and anthropic accused alibaba's qwen lab of an 'illicit' model-distillation campaign (~28.8m interactions via ~25k fake accounts), sending alibaba's hk shares ~-4.5%. for us this is a climate signal : client scrutiny is shifting from 'is ai spend justified?' to 'show me the roi, the unit economics, and the ip provenance.' sharpen your edge: today's throughline is stop translating between the tool that designs and the tool that ships . figma motion deletes the after-effects-to-css hop; creator assistant deletes the 'pull analytics, guess the why' hop; meta's one-ad-many-assets deletes the build-an-ad-per-placement hop. each move collapses a handoff and hands the optimizing to the system that already has the data — then you aim the reclaimed hour at the next lever. and the market just repriced that same instinct: it has stopped paying for ai spend on faith and now wants the outcome . web-design social paid news [{"h":"web design — run it today: animate on the figma canvas with figma motion, then copy the css/react easing from dev mode (no after effects)","blocks":[{"sub":"figma shipped figma motion at config 2026 (jun 24 2026): a real animation timeline as a fourth canvas mode (design / draw / dev / motion) — keyframe position/scale/rotation/opacity, fade/move/scale presets, custom easing as reusable motion variables, scrub-to-preview, export mp4/webm/gif/svg — and a dev mode → motion tab that copies the exact timing + easing as css, json, or framework-ready react (motion.dev)","tags":["web-design","figma","motion","animation","timeline","dev-mode"],"p":"what changed: at config 2026 on jun 24 2026 figma shipped figma motion , a real animation timeline that lives on the design canvas as a fourth mode alongside design, draw and dev. you keyframe position, scale, rotation and opacity independently, drag layers to retime them, and either set keyframes by hand on the playhead or let auto-keyframing record changes as you scrub. for a fast start there are fade / move / scale presets you stack to play together or sequence; custom easing curves can be saved as motion variables so every animation that references one updates at once. you scrub the timeline to preview and can leave time-based comments on a specific moment. it exports to mp4, webm, gif and (animated) svg . the handoff payoff: in dev mode a motion tab surfaces every timing value, easing curve and keyframe and lets a developer copy the animation as css, json, or framework-ready react (motion.dev). why it matters for us: micro-interaction polish — button hovers, modal entrances, page transitions — is on nearly every site we ship, and today it means rebuilding the design in after effects/principle/framer and then hand-translating the result into code, with the classic 'what easing did the designer actually mean?' loss at handoff. motion lets the designer animate where they already design, and the dev copy the exact curve from the same file — collapsing the translation step that quietly eats hours and introduces drift.","doit":"(1) open a file you can edit , select the frame to animate, and switch it to motion mode (next to design/draw/dev) — the timeline appears alongside the canvas. (2) fast start: apply a preset ( fade , move , or scale ); stack several on the timeline to play together, or drag to sequence. (3) refine: move the playhead and keyframe position / scale / rotation / opacity independently, or rely on auto-keyframing ; drag layers on the timeline to adjust timing. (4) reuse easing: save a custom curve as a motion variable and reference it everywhere. (5) preview by scrubbing; leave a time-based comment to flag a moment for a teammate. (6) hand off: open dev mode → motion and copy the animation as css (or json / react), or export the clip — then validate the exported easing/keyframes against your real front-end before shipping. card: card-webdesign-figma-motion.","note":"corroboration: confirmed (3) — figma's own blog ('introducing figma motion' + the config 2026 recap, primary/vendor) plus two independent press confirmations (techcrunch and the next web both on the jun 24 config 2026 animations + code-layers news). honest scope: motion is open beta , available on all plans , but starter exports are limited and full design-system integration + agent-assisted motion generation favor paid seats — so treat it as a workflow you can start today, with export gating to check on free plans. the dev mode css/react copy is vendor-claimed fidelity; eyeball the exported curve before trusting it on the critical path. distinct from code layers ('code on the canvas'), which is not a run-it-today play — it begins rolling out in july 2026 via a waitlist. joins the design-tooling/handoff branch (related to the figma dev mode mcp design↔code card and the scroll-triggered-animations css card — the same 'design→production motion' story). logged a prediction (a 2nd major design tool ships an on-canvas animation timeline that exports production motion code, or figma motion exits open beta to ga). sources: figma blog ×2, techcrunch, the next web."}]},{"h":"social (organic) — run it today: ask facebook's free creator assistant why your best reel won, then have it pitch 3 angles off live trends","blocks":[{"sub":"meta's creator assistant — a free conversational ai built into the facebook creator dashboard (live since jun 4 2026; meta doubled down jun 24 with a standalone creator studio app built on the same assistant) — answers questions against your own account data (why a post popped, when to post, how your audience shifted, what comments say) and brainstorms content ideas off live facebook trends","tags":["social","facebook","meta","creators","ai-assistant","organic"],"p":"what changed / the technique: meta's creator assistant , a free conversational ai for organic creators built into the facebook creator dashboard , went live on jun 4 2026 for creators in the us, canada and india — and on jun 24 2026 meta doubled down, announcing a standalone creator studio companion app built around the same assistant (the app itself is in limited testing / waitlist; the assistant is the part you can use now). the assistant answers questions against your own account performance — why a specific reel outperformed, when your audience is most active, how your audience has shifted over time, what people are saying in your comments — and brainstorms fresh content angles off live facebook trends (trending audio, cultural moments, top-performing content styles), tailored to your niche, with conversational follow-ups. why it matters for us: most 'ai for social' advice is a blank chatgpt prompt that knows nothing about your account, or a generic best-time-to-post chart. a first-party coach that sees your real numbers turns 'i think the hook worked' into a diagnosed, repeatable variable — and one diagnosed win, drilled into hook/length/angle variations, becomes a week of posts. it's free, organic (no ad spend), and lives next to where the content already posts.","doit":"(1) check eligibility: you need a creator account in the us, canada or india ; open the facebook mobile app → creator dashboard and look for the assistant (no separate download). (2) diagnose a winner: ask 'why did [this specific reel] outperform the rest?' and extract the repeatable variable (hook, length, audio, topic). (3) read your audience: ask 'how has my audience changed over time?' and 'what are people saying in my comments?' to find a new segment or sentiment to lean into. (4) time it: ask 'when should i post?' — it answers from your own engagement patterns, not a generic chart. (5) brainstorm off trends: ask for fresh angles tied to what's trending for your niche; capture 3. (6) iterate: follow up ('give me 3 hook variations for that angle') to spin one insight into a week of posts; a/b the cuts and export a brand pdf in edits (card-social-edits-creator-analytics). card: card-social-facebook-creator-assistant.","note":"corroboration: confirmed (3) — meta newsroom (about.fb.com, primary, jun 4) + techcrunch (jun 4 launch and jun 24 companion-app coverage) + social media today (jun 24 creator studio relaunch). honest scope: geo-gated — live only in us/canada/india right now (more markets 'over the coming months'), so verify region before relying on it; it lives in the facebook creator dashboard (not inside instagram, despite some headlines conflating the two); the standalone creator studio app is waitlist-only (the assistant is the run-it-today piece, the app is not). meta describes the access path and example questions but doesn't publish a numbered click-path, so the exact prompts above are assembled from meta's described capabilities — adapt to what your dashboard surfaces. advances the instagram/creator-tooling thread (the editor/dashboard becomes the analytics-and-ideas desk); related: card-social-edits-creator-analytics, card-social-instagram-your-algorithm, card-social-repurpose-claude. logged a prediction (creator assistant expands beyond us/ca/india or the standalone creator studio app exits waitlist by q3-end). sources: meta newsroom, techcrunch ×2, social media today."}]},{"h":"paid — run it today: pack one meta ad with up to 10 mixed image + video assets across aspect ratios, and let advantage+ pick the winner per placement","blocks":[{"sub":"meta's refreshed multimedia-ads guidance (reported jun 24 2026, from meta's own 'multi-media ads' help doc): stop splitting creative into a separate ad per placement — upload up to 10 images and videos, including multiple aspect ratios (9:16 + 1:1/4:5), into a single ad and let advantage+ delivery optimize which variation each user sees","tags":["paid","meta-ads","advantage-plus","creative","multimedia"],"p":"what changed / the technique: meta published refreshed best-practice guidance for its multimedia (multi-media) ads , written up jun 24 2026 and traced to meta's own help doc. the core instruction: upload all your assets into a single ad and let meta handle delivery automatically — specifically up to 10 images and videos per ad , and you can include assets in different aspect ratios (e.g. 1:1 and 9:16) in that same ad. meta's stated logic: 'the more creative options you provide, the more opportunities the delivery system has to optimize your delivery and performance,' picking the best-fitting variation per placement and user. why it matters for us: operators still routinely build a separate ad per aspect ratio/placement, which splinters the conversion signal across many ad objects and fragments delivery. consolidating diverse assets into one ad concentrates that signal and hands meta's andromeda-era delivery a richer combination space to optimize against — and it shifts the lever back to the thing you actually control: creative volume and variety , not audience micro-targeting. this is a creative-architecture move (how you pack assets inside the ad), distinct from the advantage+ campaign-consolidation play (how you structure the campaign).","doit":"(1) in ads manager , create/open an ad and choose the advantage+ / multimedia creative setup. (2) at the ad (creative) level, upload up to 10 assets into the one ad — deliberately mix at least one video with several images. (3) include multiple aspect ratios in that single ad : 9:16 (vertical — most inventory) plus 1:1 or 4:5 for feed. (4) make the assets conceptually distinct, not minor variants — e.g. a ugc clip, a product demo, a testimonial, a text-led explainer, a lifestyle shot (~5 genuinely different angles beats 50 near-duplicates). (5) keep advantage+ creative delivery on so meta auto-selects per placement/user; design within placement safe zones so key visuals survive cropping. (6) if any asset is ai-generated, set meta's ai-content disclosure before launch; refresh assets every 2–4 weeks , watching cpm/reach as the fatigue signal. card: card-paid-meta-multimedia-creative.","note":"corroboration: reported (2) — meta's own 'multi-media ads' help doc (primary; the page loads and matches, though its body text wasn't machine-extractable) + social media today (jun 24, which quotes the doc for the '10 assets / mixed aspect ratios' figure); the broader 'diverse creative volume, let delivery optimize' strategy is independently echoed by agency sources (admove), pushing the general play toward confirmed. honest scope: the hard figure is up to 10 assets per ad — don't conflate it with the larger asset-volume targets agencies recommend at the ad-set level (those are recommendations, not a meta limit); this is creative best-practice guidance, not a dated product launch; and ai-content disclosure has been required since mar 2026. advances the platform-ai-defaults thread (related: card-paid-meta-advantage-plus, card-paid-ai-ad-creative, card-graphic-firefly-brand-batch-creatives). logged a prediction (meta raises the per-ad asset cap above 10 or makes multimedia/multi-aspect-ratio the default creative setup). sources: meta for business help, social media today, admove. scan-line (not a card): google began rolling out the june 2026 spam update globally jun 24 (organic seo — watch search console for ranking shifts), and is testing us-only auto-applied 'strongest match' labels on search ads (no advertiser controls yet — a watch-line, not runnable)."}]},{"h":"news — the ai landscape (cross-checked; all market figures directional, premarket/intraday vs close vary by source)","blocks":[{"sub":"'ai capex is paying off' rebound flips the jun 22–23 fear: micron blew out fiscal q3 (rev ~$41.5b vs ~$35.7b est, record 84.9% gross margin, q4 guide ~$50b; hbm sold out through 2027) and jumped ~+16% premarket jun 25 — and morgan stanley reframed the selloff as a 'crowded dip, not broken roi,' resolving our jun 23 prediction ✅","tags":["news","markets","ai-tooling"],"p":"what moved: this is decisively an 'ai-capex-is-paying-off' rebound week , flipping the jun 22–23 global tech rout. micron reported fiscal q3 2026 revenue of ~$41.5b (vs ~$35.7b expected, ~16% beat), adjusted eps ~$25.1 (vs ~$20.5), a record 84.9% gross margin , and guided q4 to ~$50b revenue / ~86% margin — with data-center revenue ~$25b in the quarter, hbm4 shipments already >$1b , and hbm (high-bandwidth memory, the chips that feed ai accelerators) sold out through 2027 into 2028; the stock jumped ~+16% premarket jun 25 (directional; after-hours prints ranged +13–15%). on the chip side, openai + broadcom unveiled 'jalapeño' (jun 24), openai's first custom ai accelerator — a reticle-sized asic purpose-built for llm inference (not training), tsmc 3nm , systolic-array design with eight hbm stacks , engineering samples already running workloads, initial deployment targeted for end-2026 . and qualcomm announced an all-stock acquisition of modular (~$3.9b; the mojo/max ai-compiler company founded by llvm/swift creator chris lattner — an explicit play at nvidia's cuda software moat, not a chip startup as some feeds framed it). the repricing question itself got answered: morgan stanley (andrew slimmon, jun 24) framed the jun 22–23 drop as ai beneficiaries being 'not expensive, but crowded' with the 'earnings-revision story validated' — a major sell-side note defending the ai thesis as a crowded dip, not broken roi. why it matters for us: this is a climate signal , not a trade. two weeks ago the market punished ai on 'is the spend justified?'; this week the supply chain is visibly monetizing it. client scrutiny shifts accordingly — from 'why are we spending on ai?' to 'show me the roi, the unit economics (cost per outcome / perf-per-watt), and the ip provenance.' prediction resolved ✅ [news, made 2026-06-23, due ~jul 7]: 'within ~2 weeks, ≥1 us ai-megacap team or major sell-side note explicitly defends ai-capex roi in response to the jun 22–23 selloff' — the morgan stanley note (with micron's call as supporting evidence) clears it. corroboration: micron beat + guide confirmed (≥3 — investing.com earnings-call transcripts, futurum, cnbc); jalapeño confirmed (≥5 — openai, broadcom investor release, techcrunch, cnbc, siliconangle); qualcomm–modular confirmed (≥3 — yahoo finance, network world, wired); morgan stanley note (yahoo finance). all percentages directional. sources: micron transcript (investing.com), broadcom/openai, cnbc, yahoo finance."},{"sub":"counter-notes keep it honest: cerebras sank on a soft margin guide (ceo says investors 'misunderstood' it), oracle shed ~21,000 roles over the year (its 10-k ties cuts to ai), and anthropic accused alibaba's qwen lab of an 'illicit' model-distillation campaign — sending alibaba's hk shares ~-4.5% and surfacing ai-ip provenance as a live risk","tags":["news","ai-tooling","markets"],"p":"the rebound is selective, not blanket. cerebras (nasdaq: cbrs, ipo'd may 2026) posted first-as-public earnings — revenue $193m (+94% yoy) , loss narrowed — but the stock fell ~17–20% (directional) because fy gross-margin guidance of 38–41% undershot its ~47% q1 core margin; ceo andrew feldman told cnbc investors 'misunderstood' the guide, attributing it to temporarily renting back its own systems from a big customer while it builds capacity, not pricing pressure. oracle 's annual 10-k shows headcount down to 141,000 from ~162,000 — roughly 21,000 roles (~13%) cut over fiscal 2026 — with the filing explicitly tying reductions to 'adoption and deployment of ai technologies,' even as capex surged ~162% to $55.7b for ai data-center buildout (the 'ai pays off for owners, not always for workers' edge of the same story). and on ai-ip: anthropic accused operators linked to alibaba's qwen lab of the largest known 'distillation' campaign against it — ~ 28.8m interactions via ~25,000 fraudulent accounts (apr 22–jun 5) to 'illicitly' extract claude's coding/agentic-reasoning capability — in a letter to us senators and the white house; alibaba's hk shares (9988.hk) fell ~4.5% (directional) to a ~16-month low (ytd ~-33%), and alibaba had not commented as of jun 25 (these are anthropic's allegations, untested). why it matters for us: 'distillation' (training a cheaper model on a frontier model's outputs) becoming a public, geopolitical fight is the ip-provenance lesson going mainstream — expect clients (and procurement) to start asking where a model's capability came from and whether your ai deliverables are built on a defensible base. thread note: the v-shape 'dip not regime change' call [news, due ~jul 20] is now leaning held — the asian ai basket sits well above its jun 8 reference (samsung ~+21%, sk hynix ~+53% to a record; softbank the lone laggard ~-7%); still open. apple [due ~jul 21] is still below its jun 8 pre-wwdc level (~-3%) — open, currently trailing. corroboration: cerebras confirmed (≥3 — techcrunch, cnbc, investing.com); oracle confirmed (≥3 — yahoo finance, cnbc, 10-k); anthropic–alibaba confirmed (≥3 — reuters, investing.com, bloomberg/cnbc), allegations untested. sources: techcrunch, yahoo finance, reuters."}]}]

title: Daily Briefing — Tuesday, June 23
q: daily briefing — tuesday, june 23 tuesday, june 23, 2026 web-dev play of the day: ship realtime — live chat, presence cursors, token-by-token ai streaming — straight from your existing vercel functions, with no separate always-on backend. on jun 22 2026 vercel moved websocket support into public beta : a function can now hold a two-way websocket open using standard libraries ( ws , socket.io, hono, express) with no extra config — the connection pins to one fluid-compute instance and bills on active cpu , so an idle open socket costs almost nothing. this closes a years-old gap that used to force a second box (render/railway/a dedicated socket.io server) onto every realtime build. run-it-today: add one api/ws.ts that exports an http server with a websocketserver , move shared room/presence state into redis (instances aren't sticky), add client reconnect-with-backoff, deploy. (public beta; next.js needs the experimental experimental_upgradewebsocket() api.) email play (a 10-minute dns edit): close the phantom-subdomain spoofing hole that can quietly torch your sending reputation — add dmarcbis's new np=reject tag. dmarcbis , the long-awaited dmarc upgrade, is now a real standard — rfc 9989 (2026), which obsoletes the 2015 rfc 7489. its headline operator change is the np= tag (non-existent-subdomain policy): set np=reject and inboxes reject mail from subdomains that don't exist in your dns — the exact marketing-typo.yourbrand.com phantoms spoofers ride to borrow your domain's reputation. it also retires pct , rf and ri (use t=y for testing). with gmail/yahoo/microsoft now rejecting non-compliant bulk mail outright, this is prerequisite-grade hygiene before any bimi logo play — one record edit. (honored by the big receivers; smaller/older providers may still fall back to sp= / p= .) ai-tooling play: stop pressing "go" — define an agent loop once and let claude code run it unattended. two commands already shipping in claude code turn babysitting into automation: /loop re-runs a prompt on a schedule (give it 5m for a cron interval, or no interval to let claude self-pace 1-min–1-hr between checks), and /goal keeps a session working across turns until a separate, cheap evaluator model (defaults to haiku) confirms your condition holds — e.g. "all tests in test/auth pass and lint is clean." the technique (claire vo's jun 17 "loop engineering," echoed by addy osmani) is just: write the loop once, walk away. run-it-today: /goal implement this design doc until the acceptance tests pass or stop after 20 turns , or drop a .claude/loop.md with your daily pr-watch instructions and trigger a bare /loop . (session-scoped: fires only while claude code is open; recurring tasks expire after 7 days — for machine-off scheduling, graduate to routines/github actions.) also moved — the ai landscape (cross-checked; figures directional): a us megacap wobble mon jun 22 spilled into a violent asia session tue jun 23 — south korea's kospi fell ~8–10% (tripping circuit breakers twice), with sk hynix and samsung down double digits and foreigners net-selling ~₩5.8t (~$3.8b); nikkei ~-3%, nasdaq futures ~-2.5%. the why is two ai-specific scares, not a rates-only move: spacex fell ~16% mon (worst day since its jun 12 ipo; ~$400b wiped) after confirming a $20b bond sale to repay xai-acquisition financing — read as cash-hungry ai capex — and alphabet dropped ~5% on two marquee ai departures (gemini's noam shazeer → openai; deepmind's john jumper → anthropic). for us this is a climate signal : clients will scrutinize ai work on two fronts — "is the spend justified?" and "is the moat/talent real?" — so lead pitches with cost-per-outcome and durability, not scale. (also: openai's daybreak open-source-security initiative reported 51 issues found / 19 fixed in week one, single-source; the eu's €3 small-parcel customs duty on sub-€150 non-eu consignments goes live jul 1 .) sharpen your edge: today's throughline is delete the standing thing you babysit — a box, a hole, a key-press . you no longer need a separate server just to hold a websocket, a human in the loop to re-run the tests, or an open door for spoofers on subdomains you never created. and the market just repriced that same instinct at scale: it has stopped paying for ai spend on faith and now wants the outcome . define the loop, close the gap, prove the payoff — then aim the reclaimed hour at the next lever. web-dev email ai-tooling news [{"h":"web dev — run it today: add realtime (chat, presence, ai streaming) to your existing vercel functions with websocket support — no separate backend","blocks":[{"sub":"vercel moved websocket support for functions into public beta (jun 22 2026): a function can hold a two-way websocket open via standard libraries (ws, socket.io, hono, express) with no extra config — the connection pins to one fluid-compute instance and bills on active cpu (idle is near-free) — closing the years-old gap that forced a second always-on box onto every realtime build","tags":["web-dev","vercel","websockets","realtime","serverless","fluid-compute"],"p":"what changed: on jun 22 2026 vercel moved websocket support for vercel functions into public beta . a function can now hold a bidirectional websocket connection open using standard node libraries — ws , socket.io, hono, express, nitro/nuxt-native — with no additional configuration : a websocket is just an http get carrying an upgrade header, which vercel pins to a single fluid-compute function instance for the life of the connection. billing runs on active cpu pricing , so you pay only while the function is actually processing messages, not for idle connection time. this closes a gap that stood for years: websockets were previously impossible on vercel's serverless platform, which is why every realtime feature meant standing up a second service. why it matters for us: realtime — live chat, presence cursors, collaborative editing, token-by-token ai streaming — has been the #1 reason to bolt an always-on backend (render, railway, a dedicated socket.io box) onto an otherwise-static client build. you can now keep the whole project on vercel and add a single api/ws.ts file, deleting a moving part out of every realtime quote — and the idle-time pricing means a chat widget that sits open all day doesn't bleed compute the way a long-lived container does.","doit":"(1) confirm fluid compute is on: it's the default for projects created on/after apr 23 2025; for older projects, vercel dashboard → your project → settings → functions → fluid compute → enable . (2) install the lib: npm i ws (or npm i socket.io socket.io-client ). (3) create api/ws.ts that builds an http server and export default s it: const server = http.createserver(); const wss = new websocketserver({ server }); wss.on('connection', ws => ws.on('message', d => ws.send(d))); export default server; . (4) add client reconnect logic — connections close when the function hits its max duration, so wrap new websocket('wss://your-domain.com/api/ws') in a connect() that re-fires on close with exponential backoff (cap ~30s). (5) move shared state out of memory: instances aren't sticky and a new deploy spins fresh ones, so put rooms/presence/pub-sub in redis from the vercel marketplace , never in-process variables. (6) deploy and test with wscat -c wss://your-domain.com/api/ws ; for next.js use the documented experimental_upgradewebsocket() from @vercel/functions inside an app/api/ws/route.ts get handler. card: card-webdev-vercel-websockets.","note":"grounding: primary, single-vendor — vercel's own changelog (the public-beta announcement), the vercel docs websockets-on-functions page (exact api/ws.ts + next.js/hono/express/socket.io examples), and the vercel kb guide; three first-party vercel pages (not independent corroboration), so this is a vendor-confirmed feature, not a 3-source claim. honest scope: this is public beta (expect rough edges + possible limit changes); it requires fluid compute (default only for projects created on/after apr 23 2025); next.js needs the experimental experimental_upgradewebsocket api; there are no sticky sessions / no guaranteed instance affinity , so externalize all shared state or in-memory rooms silently break across reconnects and deploys; and chatty connections still bill real bytes (active cpu + data transfer) — idle is cheap, busy is not. joins the vercel-platform thread; related to card-webdev-vercel-cancelable-jobs and card-webdev-vercel-workflow-nitro. logged a prediction (websocket support reaches ga + next.js drops the experimental_ prefix by q3-end). sources: vercel changelog, vercel docs, vercel kb."}]},{"h":"email — run it today: close the phantom-subdomain spoofing hole with dmarcbis's new np=reject tag (rfc 9989)","blocks":[{"sub":"dmarc is now a full ietf standard — rfc 9989 (2026) obsoletes the 2015 rfc 7489 — and its headline operator change is the np= tag (non-existent-subdomain policy): np=reject makes inboxes reject mail from subdomains that don't exist in your dns (the phantoms spoofers ride to borrow your reputation), while pct/rf/ri are retired (use t=y for testing) — a 10-minute dns edit, prerequisite-grade before any bimi play","tags":["email","deliverability","dmarc","authentication","spoofing","dns"],"p":"what changed / the technique: dmarc is no longer an informal 2015 spec. the ietf published rfc 9989 (the long-awaited dmarcbis upgrade), which obsoletes the original rfc 7489 and rfc 9091 . the headline operator-facing change is a new np= tag (non-existent-subdomain policy) that lets you set none / quarantine / reject specifically for subdomains that don't exist in dns — the exact phantom subdomains ( marketing-typo.yourbrand.com ) that spammers spoof to ride on your domain's reputation. rfc 9989 also moves pct , rf and ri to historic (deprecated) status, replacing percentage-rollout with a cleaner t=y testing flag. why it matters for us: gmail, yahoo and microsoft already reject non-compliant bulk mail at the smtp level in 2026, so your deliverability — and your subscriber ltv — ride on domain auth being airtight. a spoofer exploiting a subdomain you never created can torch your domain reputation and land your real campaigns in spam; adding np=reject closes that hole in one dns edit, and it's a prerequisite-grade move before any bimi logo play. ten minutes, outsized protection.","doit":"(1) pull your current record: run dig txt _dmarc.yourbrand.com +short (or a free dmarc lookup) and copy the existing string. (2) confirm your base policy is enforcing — np only takes effect at the organizational domain, so make sure you already have p=quarantine or p=reject (if you're still on p=none , keep monitoring first, but you can safely add np now). (3) add the new tag: edit the _dmarc txt record to append np=reject — e.g. v=dmarc1; p=quarantine; np=reject; rua=mailto:reports@yourbrand.com; fo=1 (this rejects non-existent-subdomain mail while leaving your real sending subdomains under p / sp ). (4) remove deprecated tags: delete any pct= , rf= or ri= ; if you used pct= for a staged rollout, switch to t=y (testing mode). (5) publish and validate the record through a dmarc checker so it parses cleanly. (6) watch your rua aggregate reports for 3–7 days to confirm legitimate subdomain mail still authenticates before tightening p further. card: card-email-dmarcbis-np.","note":"corroboration: confirmed (4) — rfc 9989 (primary), plus validity, dmarcwise and suped independently confirming the np tag, the deprecation of pct / rf / ri , and the rfc 7489/9091 obsoletion. honest scope: this is a standards-level change, not an esp product drop (rfc 9989 finalized in 2026) — genuinely actionable now, but it's a spec to apply at your dns, not a button in your esp; np is honored by the big receivers but not universally (older/smaller providers may still fall back to sp= / p= ); the record still begins v=dmarc1 (there is no v=dmarcbis — don't change the version); and bimi still requires p=quarantine / reject separately. advances the email deliverability thread (distinct from gmail's postmaster verdict and the third-party ipr seed test); related: card-email-dmarc-bimi, card-email-postmaster-deliverability-analysis, card-email-inbox-placement-audit. logged a prediction (a major mailbox provider references rfc 9989 / np in sender guidance by q3-end). sources: rfc 9989, validity, dmarcwise, suped."}]},{"h":"ai tooling — run it today: define an agent loop once and let claude code run it unattended with /loop and /goal","blocks":[{"sub":"two commands already in claude code turn babysitting into automation: /loop re-runs a prompt on a schedule (a cron interval like 5m, or self-paced when you give none) and /goal keeps a session working across turns until a separate cheap evaluator model (defaults to haiku) confirms your 'done when' condition holds — the runnable substrate behind the jun 17 'loop engineering' technique (claire vo / chatprd, echoed by addy osmani)","tags":["ai-tooling","claude-code","agents","automation","loops","autonomous"],"p":"what changed / the technique: on jun 17 2026 claire vo (chatprd) published a "how i ai" episode and companion lenny's newsletter post on loop engineering — designing autonomous agent loops instead of hand-prompting an agent turn by turn. the runnable substrate is already shipping in claude code. /loop re-runs a prompt on a cron interval (or a claude-chosen one), and /goal keeps the session working across turns until a fresh, separate evaluator model confirms a condition holds (e.g. "all tests in test/auth pass and lint is clean"). addy osmani independently codified the same pattern (jun 7) into four loop types — heartbeat, cron, hook, goal — plus worktrees, skills and verification subagents. the net: you stop being the person who presses "go" repeatedly and instead define the loop once. why it matters for us: the team burns real hours babysitting deploys, chasing stale prs and re-running test/lint cycles by hand — exactly the toil these loops absorb. a /goal loop with a separate evaluator (it defaults to haiku , so checking is cheap) turns "implement this design doc until the acceptance criteria pass" into a walk-away task — a multiplier you can wire up today with zero new tools or infra, just commands already in the cli.","doit":"(1) confirm you're current: claude --version — /loop needs v2.1.72+, /goal needs v2.1.139+; accept the workspace-trust dialog ( /goal runs through the hooks system). (2) quick poll loop: type /loop 5m check if the deploy finished and tell me what happened — claude converts 5m to cron, schedules it, returns a job id (press esc to stop while it waits). (3) self-paced loop: type /loop check whether ci passed and address any review comments (no interval) — claude picks a 1-min-to-1-hr delay per iteration and can end itself once provably done. (4) work-until-done goal: type /goal all tests in test/auth pass and the lint step is clean or stop after 20 turns — a ◎ /goal active indicator shows runtime, a small fast model checks the condition after each turn and feeds any "no" back as guidance (bare /goal shows turns/tokens; /goal clear aborts). (5) make a reusable default loop: create .claude/loop.md with your maintenance instructions ("check the release pr; if ci is red, pull the failing log, diagnose, push a minimal fix") so a bare /loop runs it. (6) manage jobs in plain english ("what scheduled tasks do i have?" / "cancel the deploy check"); for schedule-sensitive runs use a minute that isn't :00 / :30 to dodge the jitter offset. card: card-ai-tooling-claude-loop-goal.","note":"corroboration: confirmed (4) — the official claude code docs (scheduled-tasks + goal pages, primary), lenny's newsletter/claire vo, and chatprd (addy osmani's loop-engineering post corroborates independently). honest scope: /loop and /goal are session-scoped — they fire only while claude code is running and idle, recurring tasks auto-expire 7 days after creation, and there's no catch-up for missed fires (a fresh conversation clears tasks; --resume restores unexpired ones). the /goal evaluator judges only what claude surfaces in the transcript — it doesn't run commands or read files itself — so conditions must be provable from claude's own output. for unattended, machine-off scheduling you graduate to routines (anthropic cloud, 1-hour minimum), desktop scheduled tasks, or github actions; some bedrock/vertex behaviors differ. advances the multi-agent-orchestration thread (now a cross-tool storyline with codex's /goal ); related: card-ai-tooling-codex-goal, card-ai-tooling-claude-workflows. logged a prediction (anthropic relaxes the 7-day expiry or ships an in-cli renew by q3-end). sources: claude code docs (scheduled-tasks), claude code docs (goal), lenny's newsletter, chatprd."}]},{"h":"news — the ai landscape (cross-checked; all market figures directional, intraday vs close varies by source)","blocks":[{"sub":"global tech rout: a us megacap wobble mon jun 22 spilled into asia tue jun 23 — kospi ~-8–10% (circuit breakers tripped twice), sk hynix & samsung down double digits, foreigners net-selling ~₩5.8t (~$3.8b); the trigger was two ai-specific scares — spacex's $20b bond (capex cash-hunger; ~-16%, ~$400b wiped) and alphabet's ai-talent exits (~-5%)","tags":["news","markets","ai-tooling"],"p":"what moved: a us megacap tech wobble on mon jun 22 spilled into a violent asia session on tue jun 23 , hammering the ai/semiconductor complex. south korea's kospi fell on the order of 8–10% (korea times put the close at -9.99% , 8,203.84; yahoo captured intraday -8.11% ), tripping circuit breakers twice ; sk hynix ~-11.5 to -12.5% and samsung ~-8.8 to -12.3% ; the nikkei ~-3% (snapping an 8-session streak) and nasdaq futures ~-2.5% ; foreign investors net-sold ~ ₩5.79t (~$3.8b) of korean shares. the why is two ai-specific triggers plus a structural amplifier, not a rates-only move. (1) ai-capex confidence shock — spacex fell ~16.4% mon (worst day since its jun 12 ipo) after confirming a $20b bond sale to repay bridge financing from its xai acquisition — read as a cash-hungry ai-infrastructure signal that wiped ~ $400b in value and "rattled confidence in ai capital spending." (2) ai-talent / commoditization fear — alphabet closed ~-5% (intraday as much as -7.2%), its worst day in over a year, after two high-profile departures: gemini co-lead noam shazeer → openai and deepmind vp / nobel laureate john jumper (alphafold) → anthropic . (3) structural amplifier: taiwan/korea/japan benchmarks were each up 40%+ ytd on a concentrated semiconductor trade — the most exposed names when us megacap sentiment turned; korean analysts (kiwoom) framed it as profit-taking / short-term correction layered with rate-hike jitters. net read: an ai-capex-scrutiny + talent-flight + crowded-trade unwind. why it matters for us: this is a climate signal , not a trade — the market just punished ai on the two questions clients will now mirror, "is the spend justified?" (spacex/capex) and "is the talent/moat real?" (alphabet exits). frame ai pitches around defensible, cost-per-outcome results and durability, and expect "is this a bubble?" pushback in the room. thread impact: this pressures but does not resolve our open "dip not regime change" v-shape call — the basket (softbank, samsung, sk hynix) now sits well below its jun 8 reference, so the bar to clear it by the ~jul 20 due date just rose; still open. corroboration: selloff size/scope confirmed (≥3 — yahoo finance, korea times, bloomberg, cnbc); spacex $20b bond / ~-16% / ~$400b confirmed (≥3 — yahoo, beincrypto, techtimes, cnbc); alphabet ~-5% + the two exits confirmed (≥3 — bloomberg, yahoo, cnbc). all percentages directional, intraday vs close vary by source. sources: yahoo finance, korea times, cnbc."},{"sub":"also: openai's daybreak open-source-security initiative (week one: 51 issues found, 19 fixed across 19 projects — single-source) and the eu's €3 small-parcel customs duty on sub-€150 non-eu consignments goes live jul 1 2026 (confirmed)","tags":["news","ai-tooling","regulation"],"p":"two smaller landscape markers. openai daybreak — a cyber-defense program run with trail of bits — reported its first week of "patch the planet": across 19 open-source projects it found 51 issues and had 19 already fixed , pitching ai that bakes defense into software from the start and cuts analysis time "hours to minutes" (engadget, jun 23 — single-source on the specifics, treat the counts as reported/directional). separately, the eu's €3 small-parcel customs duty goes live jul 1 2026 : non-eu consignments under €150 carry a fixed €3 customs duty (applied per tariff heading, collected via ioss), with a separate ~€2 handling fee still under negotiation — directly relevant to any client running cross-border dtc/ecommerce into the eu (eu council/consilium + kpmg + vat trade press, confirmed). why it matters for us: the daybreak data reinforces the "treat your toolchain/dependencies as an attack surface" lesson we keep teaching on the dev side; the eu duty is a concrete july cost change to flag in any cross-border commerce build or pitch. sources: engadget, eu council (consilium)."}]}]

title: Daily Briefing — Sunday, June 21
q: daily briefing — sunday, june 21 sunday, june 21, 2026 web-design play of the day: do scroll-reveal animations in pure css — fire an animation once an element enters the viewport, no intersectionobserver, no javascript. chrome shipped scroll- triggered animations (chrome 145/146, 2026) — the missing half of scroll animation. scroll- driven animation scrubs an animation to your scroll position; scroll- triggered plays a normal, timed animation once when the element crosses a threshold (think css's own intersection observer). two new properties do it: timeline-trigger: --t view() entry 100% exit 0% defines when it fires, and animation-trigger: --t play-forwards ties your @keyframes to that trigger. action keywords cover the lot — play-forwards , play-backwards , play-once , pause , reset , replay . it's chromium-only today (firefox/safari not yet), so keep your intersectionobserver as the fallback and treat it as progressive enhancement — but it deletes a whole category of reveal-on-scroll js for most of your traffic. graphic play: re-direct footage you already shot — change the camera angle, swap the environment, or restyle a clip from a reference image — inside figma, no reshoot. on jun 18 2026 figma put runway's aleph 2.0 model into figma weave : frame-level video direction on the design canvas. it edits clips up to 30 seconds (a full scene, not a 2-second sample), takes a reference image to apply a look while preserving everything you didn't ask to change, and carries keyframe edits through every frame a subject appears in. the operator move: stop re-filming for every variant — drop a clip on the weave canvas, type the change, preview before you commit. (pricing is shifting to scale with input length; check current cost before batch-running client work.) growth play: stop spending time on llms.txt for ai visibility — the data says almost nobody reads it — and start tracking your real ai-citation share where you actually can. ahrefs analyzed 137,000 domains (study jun 15 2026): 97% of llms.txt files got zero requests in a month, and ai bots were ~1% of all fetches; google's john mueller says a self-reported file 'can't help an llm tell one site from another.' meanwhile bing webmaster tools shipped ai citation share (preview, global, jun 19 2026) — the percentage of ai citations your site captures for a grounding query, plus how you stack up against competitors (bing/copilot only; google search console still has no citation metric). reallocate the llms.txt hour into clean html structure, internal links, and one-sentence answers up top — then watch your citation share move. cro/aov play (answering the team's open question, sharper this time): set your free-shipping threshold by contribution margin , not a round number above aov — and give mobile its own, lower threshold. the folklore rule ('aov + 15–25%') can quietly lose money. the honest math: (threshold − aov) × gross-margin% = the extra margin a nudged order earns ; your threshold makes sense only when that covers the shipping you're giving away. and because mobile aov runs ~20–30% lower than desktop , one blended threshold leaves phone shoppers staring at a goal they can't reach — so set a separate, lower mobile bar. keep the live 'you're $x away' progress bar (still the highest-roi single aov widget); just stop picking the number by vibes. context: deloitte's 2026 outlook found 67% of retail execs plan to raise their threshold this year — re-peg yours to current aov and re-run the margin math. sharpen your edge: today's throughline is do less of the busywork the platform doesn't reward, and put the saved effort where the signal is . don't hand-write reveal-on-scroll js the browser now does in two css lines; don't re-shoot a clip you can re-direct on the canvas; don't tend an llms.txt file 97% of bots ignore; don't pick a shipping threshold by gut when a one-line margin formula tells you the real number. each is the same move — delete the ritual, keep the result, and aim the freed-up hour at the lever that actually pays. web-design graphic growth cro [{"h":"web design — run it today: fire reveal-on-scroll animations from pure css (no intersectionobserver) with scroll-triggered animations","blocks":[{"sub":"chrome shipped scroll-triggered animations (chrome 145/146, 2026) — the counterpart to scroll-driven animation: a normal timed animation that plays once when an element crosses a viewport threshold, declared with two new properties (timeline-trigger + animation-trigger) and zero javascript","tags":["web-design","css","animation","scroll","progressive-enhancement"],"p":"what changed: chrome shipped scroll-triggered animations (announced for chrome 145 , shipping in the 145/146 stable wave, 2026). this is the half of scroll animation that was missing. scroll-driven animation (already baseline-ish in chromium) scrubs an animation's progress to how far you've scrolled — great for progress bars and parallax. scroll-triggered animation is different: it plays an ordinary, time-based @keyframes animation once , the moment an element crosses a scroll threshold — exactly the 'reveal this card as it scrolls into view' pattern teams currently hand-build with javascript's intersectionobserver . two new css properties do the work. timeline-trigger (longhands timeline-trigger-name / timeline-trigger-source ) names a trigger and points it at a timeline — e.g. timeline-trigger: --t view() entry 100% exit 0% means 'fire when the element has fully entered the view and reset when it has fully exited.' then animation-trigger: --t play-forwards binds your animation to that trigger. the action keywords cover the full range: play-forwards , play-backwards , play-once , pause , reset , replay , none . why it matters for us: reveal-on-scroll is on nearly every marketing site we ship, and today it costs a js bundle, event wiring, and a class of bugs (observers that never disconnect, animations that re-fire on every scroll jiggle). moving it into the stylesheet makes it declarative, designer-editable, and cheaper — and because an unsupported browser simply shows the element in its final state, it degrades cleanly.","doit":"(1) write the keyframes + animation as normal: @keyframes fade-up { from { opacity:0; translate:0 1rem } to { opacity:1; translate:0 0 } } and on the element animation: fade-up .35s ease both; . (2) define the trigger: add timeline-trigger: --reveal view() entry 100% exit 0%; (the view() timeline + entry/exit range decides exactly when it fires). (3) bind the animation to it: add animation-trigger: --reveal play-forwards; — now the animation runs once when the element enters, instead of immediately on load. (4) pick the behaviour: use play-once for a one-time reveal, or play-forwards / play-backwards for enter/leave pairs. (5) keep a fallback for non-chromium: wrap the js path in @supports not (animation-trigger: --x play-forwards) (or feature-detect) and keep your existing intersectionobserver reveal for firefox/safari — or just let elements render in their final state there (no animation, no breakage). (6) try it on one section (a testimonials row or feature grid) before ripping out your animation library. card: card-webdesign-scroll-triggered-animations.","note":"grounding: the property names ( timeline-trigger / animation-trigger ), the action keywords, and the 'plays once vs scrubs' distinction are from chrome for developers' own announcement and an independent css-tricks first-look (geoff graham); utilitybend covers the same feature independently. honest scope: this is chromium-only right now — chrome for developers' demo explicitly ships an intersectionobserver fallback, and the spec is still settling (treat exact range syntax as subject to change), so ship it as progressive enhancement, not a dependency you delete for all users (that's the baseline test we keep applying). joins thread-modern-css-primitives (the chromium-only branch: carousel, sibling-index, gap-decorations). logged a prediction (a 2nd engine ships animation-trigger → baseline path). sources: chrome for developers, css-tricks, utilitybend."}]},{"h":"graphic — run it today: re-direct video you already shot (camera angle, environment, restyle from a reference) inside figma with runway aleph 2.0 in weave — no reshoot","blocks":[{"sub":"figma put runway's aleph 2.0 model into figma weave (jun 18 2026): frame-level video editing on the design canvas — edit clips up to 30s, apply a look from a reference image while preserving everything else, and carry a keyframe edit through every frame a subject appears in","tags":["graphic","video","ai-video","figma","runway","brand"],"p":"what changed: on jun 18 2026 figma integrated runway's aleph 2.0 video model into figma weave , its generative canvas — bringing 'direct every frame' editing to where brand and design work already happens. aleph 2.0 is built for control rather than one-shot generation: it edits clips up to 30 seconds (long enough to direct a full scene, not just a 2-second sample); you can bring in a reference image to inform the look and it applies that across the video while preserving everything you didn't ask to change ; and keyframe edits carry through — change a subject in one frame and the edit follows that subject wherever it appears. once a clip is on the canvas you can change the camera angle, introduce a new character, or transform the environment entirely — without a reshoot , previewing each change before you commit in a connected, iterative workflow. why it matters for us: video is now table-stakes for brand and social, and the expensive part has always been re-shooting for every variant, aspect ratio, or client note. this collapses 'we need another angle / a different background / an on-brand restyle' from a production day into a canvas edit, in the same figma file where the rest of the creative lives — so the designer, not just the editor, can iterate. (frames the durable shift: ai video is moving from 'generate a clip' to 'direct the clip you have.')","doit":"(1) open figma weave and drop your existing clip (or a generated one) onto the canvas. (2) restyle from a reference: add a reference image and prompt the look you want — aleph 2.0 applies it while keeping untouched elements intact (good for matching a campaign's grade/palette). (3) re-direct the scene: prompt a new camera angle, a swapped environment, or an added element instead of scheduling a reshoot. (4) use keyframe edits when a change needs to track a subject across the shot — set it once and let it carry through the frames. (5) preview, then commit: review each change in the connected workflow before finalizing; iterate on the parts that miss. (6) check cost first: figma says pricing is moving to scale with input length — confirm current credits/pricing before batch-running variants for a client. card: card-graphic-runway-aleph-figma-weave.","note":"corroboration: reported (2) — figma's own blog ('direct every frame with runway aleph 2.0, now in figma weave', primary) + runway's aleph 2.0 product page (independent confirmation of the model's capabilities). honest scope: pricing is explicitly in flux ('will be updated soon to scale with input length') — treat cost as a check-before-you-run item; outputs still need a human eye for artifacts and for likeness/rights when you add or alter people. confirm commercial-use terms for client deliverables. advances the graphic ai-creative thread from 'train the brand + generate' toward 'direct existing footage'; related to card-graphic-firefly-brand-batch-creatives (the brand-trained-generation play). logged a prediction (a 2nd major design/creative tool ships in-canvas frame-level ai video direction). sources: figma blog, runway."}]},{"h":"growth — run it today: drop llms.txt as an ai-visibility tactic (97% of files go unread) and track your real ai-citation share in bing webmaster tools instead","blocks":[{"sub":"new data (ahrefs, 137,000 domains, jun 15 2026): 97% of llms.txt files got zero requests in a month and ai bots were ~1% of fetches — google's mueller says a self-reported file can't differentiate sites; meanwhile bing webmaster tools shipped ai citation share (preview, global, jun 19 2026), reporting the % of ai citations your site wins for a query vs competitors","tags":["growth","aeo","ai-search","seo","measurement"],"p":"what changed: two things landed this week that re-point aeo (answer engine optimization — getting named in ai answers) effort. (1) the llms.txt myth got measured. ahrefs analyzed 137,000 domains (study published jun 15 2026 ) and found 97% of llms.txt files received zero requests in the month studied; ai retrieval bots were only ~1% of total fetches, and of the few requests that did hit those files, the large majority were generic bots, not citation engines. google's john mueller added the why: because llms.txt is self-reported, it 'can't help an llm tell one site from another,' and google's generative-ai guidance says machine-readable files like it aren't needed to appear in ai search. translation: publishing llms.txt is cheap and harmless, but it is not a lever that moves ai visibility right now — so stop budgeting time for it. (2) ai-citation measurement got more first-party. bing webmaster tools rolled out an ai citation share report (preview, global, jun 19 2026 ) inside its ai performance dashboard: it shows the percentage of ai citations your site captures for a given grounding query, plus a competitive comparison, alongside new intents and topics groupings. the catch: it covers bing/copilot answers only — google search console still surfaces no ai-citation metric. why it matters for us: this sharpens the aeo loop we've been teaching. the work that pays is clean html structure, internal links, and genuinely good one-sentence answers up top — not protocol files crawlers ignore. spend the reclaimed hour there, and use citation share (where your audience uses bing/copilot) plus the visibility checkers we already use to confirm it landed.","doit":"(1) stop investing in llms.txt for visibility: leave any existing file in place (it's harmless), but remove it from your aeo to-do list and don't sell it to clients as a ranking/citation lever. (2) turn on ai citation share: in bing webmaster tools → ai performance , open the citation share report and read your share of ai citations for your key grounding queries, plus the competitor comparison. (3) pair it with cross-engine checks: bing-only data isn't the whole picture — keep running a free ai-visibility check (card-growth-ai-visibility-check) for chatgpt/gemini/perplexity and read a few real ai answers by hand. (4) redirect the effort to what's measured to work: clean heading/html structure, strong internal links, an intent-matched title, a one-sentence answer at the top, and faq/article schema (card-growth-schema-aeo, card-growth-aeo-content-formats). (5) re-measure citation share in ~2–4 weeks to confirm your structural pass moved your share. card: card-growth-ai-visibility-check (updated).","note":"corroboration: reported (2) — search engine journal's seo pulse (covers both the bing citation share launch and the llms.txt data) + ahrefs' own 137k-domain study (primary for the 97%/1% figures). honest scope: citation share is bing/copilot-only and in preview — it is not a read on google's ai overviews/ai mode, where there's still no first-party citation metric, so don't treat it as total ai visibility. the llms.txt finding is 'not useful for citation today,' not 'will never matter' — the standard (microsoft) is young; some still publish it defensively. advances thread-answer-engine-optimization (optimize → measure → mark-up, now: don't over-invest in ignored protocol files). logged a prediction (google search console ships an ai-citation/answer-visibility metric matching bing's citation share). sources: search engine journal, ahrefs."}]},{"h":"cro & aov — run it today: set your free-shipping threshold by contribution margin (not a round number above aov), and give mobile its own lower bar","blocks":[{"sub":"the 'aov + 15–25%' folklore can quietly lose money; the honest test is (threshold − aov) × gross-margin% ≥ the shipping you give away — and because mobile aov runs ~20–30% lower than desktop, a single blended threshold leaves phone shoppers chasing a goal they rarely reach, so set a separate mobile threshold (re-answers the team's standing cro/aov question, sharper)","tags":["cro","aov","free-shipping","margin","mobile","checkout"],"p":"what changed / why resurface it: the team's open ask-anything question — 'the best one thing to focus on for cro and aov' — is back in the demand backlog, so here's the sharper answer than the first pass. the single highest-roi aov widget is still the free-shipping threshold + a live 'you're $x away' progress bar (the goal-gradient effect: people push harder as a reward gets closer). what changes today is how you pick the number . the popular rule — set the bar ~15–25% above your current aov — is a fine starting hypothesis but it ignores margin, and a threshold that lifts aov while you eat shipping can be a net loss . the honest method is contribution-margin math: (threshold − aov) × gross-margin% = the incremental margin a nudged order earns , and the threshold only makes sense when that incremental margin at least covers the per-order shipping you're now giving away. second correction: mobile aov runs roughly 20–30% lower than desktop , so one blended threshold sets a goal most phone shoppers can't reach — quietly wasting the nudge on your largest traffic source. set a separate, lower mobile threshold . why it matters for us: this is the difference between an aov tactic that reads well in a deck and one that survives a p&l. the mechanism (goal-gradient) is settled; the number is a margin calculation and a device segmentation, not a vibe. (context: deloitte's 2026 retail outlook found 67% of execs plan to raise their threshold this year against carrier/tariff cost shocks — a stale number is likely now too low and wrong on margin.)","doit":"(1) pull the inputs: last-90-day aov, gross-margin %, and your real average shipping cost per order — split by device (mobile vs desktop). (2) run the margin test, not the round number: for a candidate threshold, compute (threshold − aov) × gross-margin% and only keep thresholds where that covers the shipping you'd absorb on a nudged order. (3) set a separate mobile threshold ~20–30% below your desktop number so phone shoppers see a goal they can actually hit. (4) keep the progress bar in cart/mini-cart ('you're $12 away from free shipping') with one relevant low-cost add-on beside it — that widget is the lift; the threshold is just the target. (5) ai assist: export your order-value distribution and ask claude/chatgpt for the threshold that captures the most 'one-nudge-away' orders at or above your margin floor, per device, and to draft the bar copy. (6) measure all three for 3–4 weeks — aov, conversion, and shipping-cost margin — before locking it; re-peg annually (the 2026 reset). card: card-cro-free-shipping-threshold (updated).","note":"corroboration: the margin-math method + the mobile-vs-desktop aov gap come from digital applied's 2026 free-shipping playbook (contribution-margin framing; cites deloitte 67% and baymard ~48% surprise-cost abandonment) and growth suite's aov guide (mobile aov ~20–30% lower); baymard, capital one shopping, and deloitte back the underlying cro/aov stats. honest flag: the specific percentages (15–25% above aov; 20–30% mobile gap; ~15–20% progress-bar lift) are well-supported starting points to test on your store, not guarantees — the durable rule is 'watch the margin, not just aov/cvr.' advances the cro 'two highest-leverage levers' thread (refines the threshold method rather than repeating it); related: card-cro-show-total-cost-early, card-cro-post-purchase-upsell, card-cro-cross-sell-bundles. sources: digital applied, growth suite, baymard, capital one shopping, deloitte."}]}]

title: Daily Briefing — Saturday, June 20
q: daily briefing — saturday, june 20 saturday, june 20, 2026 web-dev play of the day: stop handing your ai coding agent a long-lived deploy key — let it ship to cloudflare with a throwaway account instead. on jun 19 2026 cloudflare shipped temporary accounts for ai agents : when an agent runs wrangler deploy with no credentials, wrangler tells it to rerun with --temporary and the worker (plus its bindings — d1, kv, r2) deploys to a fresh, isolated preview account in seconds. the agent gets short-lived creds it can redeploy with for 60 minutes ; if nobody clicks the claim link in that window, the account and everything in it is auto-deleted . the security win: an autonomous agent never holds your real api token or touches your production account, and a forgotten experiment cleans itself up. run-it-today: point your agent at wrangler deploy --temporary for prototypes, review the preview url, then claim only the ones worth keeping. (joins our 'treat the ai toolchain as an attack surface — least-privilege everything' thread.) paid play (do this before aug 18): google is about to auto-enroll you in conversion-based customer match lists and auto-label your audiences — get in front of it. google ads is automatically turning on conversion-based customer lists for advertisers already running enhanced conversions + customer match, with data processing starting aug 18 2026 ; separately it will start auto-classifying customer-list 'types' from august. the risk: lists built from your conversion data get created/relabeled without your say-so, and a mislabel can quietly skew smart bidding and targeting. the move isn't panic — it's a 10-minute audit: open tools → shared library → audience manager , review every conversion-based list, set your own classifications now, and decide per-account whether to keep the auto-enrollment (account settings → customer match → 'turn on conversion-based customer lists') or switch it off before aug 18. first-party data is the asset — own how it's grouped. social play: turn meta's edits app into your reels research-and-pitch desk — best-time-to-post, side-by-side a/b, and a brand-ready pdf in a few taps. meta's edits (its free capcut rival, update jun 17 2026) added real audience insights (when your followers are actually online), compare up to 3 reels side-by-side in-app, one-tap export of that data to a pdf for brand/partner pitches, text-prompt ai restyle of clips, and longer exports ( up to 15 min on ios). the play: stop guessing. edit in edits, post your top cut at your audience's peak window, line up your last three reels to see which hook/format actually won, and hand brands a clean pdf instead of screenshots. (ai restyle is region-limited with regeneration caps — check availability.) also moved — the ai landscape (cross-check before quoting; us markets were closed fri jun 19 for juneteenth, so the last close is thu jun 18): norway imposed a near- ban on generative ai in elementary school (grades 1–7, ages 6–13; ages 14–16 only with teacher supervision) from the new school year in late august — a notable regulatory marker on ai-in-education (reuters, engadget). apple began opening ios to third-party app stores in brazil (effective with ios 26.5 under a cade deal; 5% core technology fee, notarization required, no open web sideloading — developers must accept the new license by jul 6) — another dma-style crack in the walled garden (engadget, techcrunch, techmeme, confirmed). elastic agreed to buy crv-backed deductive ai for up to ~$85m (ai for incident investigation; techcrunch, techmeme, reported). and south korea's chip-worker mega-bonuses (samsung/sk hynix) have the bank of korea on inflation alert — a small read on how hot the memory/ai-hardware cycle is running (cnbc). sharpen your edge: today's throughline is the platform is about to act on your stuff — get ahead of the default . google will auto-build and auto-label your audiences unless you classify them first; an unattended agent will hold whatever key you give it unless you give it a disposable one; meta will rank your reels on signals it now shows you, so design for the peak-time slot and the winning hook before you post. the edge isn't reacting to the change — it's setting your own defaults in the 10 minutes before the platform sets them for you. web-dev paid social news [{"h":"web dev — run it today: let your ai coding agent deploy to cloudflare with a disposable account (no long-lived api token, auto-deletes in 60 min) using 'wrangler deploy --temporary'","blocks":[{"sub":"cloudflare shipped 'temporary accounts for ai agents' (jun 19 2026): an agent that runs wrangler with no credentials is told to rerun with --temporary, deploying the worker + its bindings to an isolated preview account it can iterate on for 60 minutes — then a human claims it, or it auto-deletes","tags":["web-dev","cloudflare","workers","ai-agents","security","least-privilege"],"p":"what changed: on jun 19 2026 cloudflare launched temporary accounts for ai agents . until now, for an ai agent to deploy a cloudflare worker someone had to first sign up, run an oauth flow in a browser, or mint an api token and hand it to the agent — friction that ends with a long-lived production credential sitting in an autonomous agent's context . the new flow removes both the friction and that risk: when an agent runs wrangler deploy with no credentials, wrangler now prints a prompt telling it to rerun with --temporary ; wrangler then spins up a fresh, isolated temporary account and deploys the worker — including its associated resources (d1 databases, kv, r2, other bindings) — to a live preview url in seconds. the agent receives short-lived credentials it can redeploy with for 60 minutes . to keep the work, a human clicks the claim link, signs in or signs up, and the temporary account and everything in it converts to a permanent account they own. the guardrail: if no one claims it within 60 minutes, the temporary account and its resources are automatically deleted. why it matters for us: this is least-privilege made the default for agentic builds. an agent prototyping a client api, a webhook, or a demo never touches your real account or your production token, and abandoned experiments garbage-collect themselves instead of accumulating as orphaned workers and leaked keys. it's the clean version of the lesson we keep teaching — never give an autonomous tool more standing access than the task needs — applied to deploy infrastructure. (part of cloudflare's broader agents-week push toward scoped/short-lived agent credentials: scannable api tokens, resource-scoped permissions ga, and workers vpc for scoped access to private dbs.)","doit":"(1) have your agent (or you) deploy with the flag: wrangler deploy --temporary — no prior login, no api token required. (2) read the output: wrangler returns the live preview url plus short-lived creds; the agent can keep iterating and redeploying for the 60-minute window. (3) review the result at the preview url before committing to anything — treat the temporary account as a sandbox. (4) claim only what's worth keeping: open the claim link, sign in/up, and convert the temporary account (worker + d1/kv/r2 bindings) into a permanent account you own. (5) let the rest expire: anything you don't claim within 60 minutes auto-deletes — no cleanup, no orphaned resources, no leaked keys. (6) make it policy: for any agent that deploys, default it to --temporary for prototypes and reserve real, scoped tokens for the few things that go to production. card: card-webdev-cloudflare-temporary-accounts.","note":"grounding: the --temporary command, the 60-minute claim-or-auto-delete window, and the 'bindings claimed together' behavior are from cloudflare's own announcement + developer changelog (both jun 19 2026). honest scope: this is cloudflare-specific (workers/wrangler) and aimed at the agentic-deploy workflow — it's a security/ergonomics primitive, not a multi-cloud standard. the broader scoped-credential pieces (scannable tokens, resource-scoped permissions ga, workers vpc) ship alongside it as part of agents week. advances the ai-toolchain-as-attack-surface thread and the least-privilege durable lesson (related: card-webdev-rotate-ai-toolchain, card-webdev-ai-gateway-spend-limits, card-webdev-flue-cloudflare-agent). logged a prediction (a 2nd major deploy platform ships disposable/short-lived agent deploy credentials). sources: cloudflare blog, cloudflare developer changelog."}]},{"h":"paid — do it before aug 18: audit and classify your customer match audiences before google auto-enrolls and auto-labels them","blocks":[{"sub":"google ads is automatically turning on conversion-based customer lists for advertisers already using enhanced conversions + customer match (data processing starts aug 18 2026), and will start auto-classifying list 'types' from august — a mislabel can quietly skew smart bidding, so set your own classifications now","tags":["paid","google-ads","customer-match","first-party-data","privacy","audiences"],"p":"what changed: google ads is rolling out two related, mostly-automatic changes to customer match . (1) it is auto-enabling 'conversion-based customer lists' for advertisers who already run enhanced conversions and customer match but haven't switched the feature on themselves — the system then auto-builds audience segments from the hashed first-party data you already collect at each conversion goal, with data processing scheduled to begin aug 18 2026 (you can turn it off before then). (2) separately, from august 2026 google will begin automatically classifying the 'customer type' of conversion-based lists (manually uploaded lists are excluded). why it matters for us: this is google pushing every advertiser toward first-party-data audiences by default — useful, but it quietly removes a layer of control. if google mislabels a list's customer type, that classification can feed smart bidding and targeting and subtly mis-steer spend without anyone noticing. the operator move is the same one we keep teaching for ai-default platform changes: don't fight the default, get in front of it — review what's about to be auto-created/relabeled and set your own classifications before the system sets them for you. (note: the auto-classification detail was first surfaced by a practitioner on linkedin, not an official google post — the auto-enrollment + aug 18 date are reported by the trade press; treat dates as directional until your in-account notice lands.)","doit":"(1) audit now: open tools → shared library → audience manager and review every conversion-based / customer match list — note which are about to be auto-created or relabeled. (2) set your own classifications on those lists before august, so google adopts your labels instead of guessing the customer type. (3) decide on the auto-enrollment per account: account settings (admin) → customer match → conversion-based customer lists → check or uncheck 'turn on conversion-based customer lists' and save — opt in if you want the free first-party audiences, opt out before aug 18 2026 if you don't want lists built automatically. (4) re-check downstream: confirm smart bidding / targeting on affected campaigns still reflects the segments you intend (a wrong customer-type label can skew bidding). (5) confirm the data plumbing: enhanced conversions must be firing correctly for these lists to be accurate — verify in conversions before relying on them. card: card-paid-google-conversion-customer-lists.","note":"corroboration: reported (2) — search engine land (auto-enrollment + aug 18 data-processing date + opt-out) and search engine roundtable independently cover the rollout; google ads help documents the feature and the exact on/off toggle. honest caveats: the auto-classification piece traces to a practitioner linkedin post amplified by the trade press, not an official google blog — so treat the 'auto-label from august' specifics and exact dates as directional until the notice appears in your account. advances the google-ads 'controls collapse into ai/automatic defaults — keep a manual hand on the wheel' thread (related: card-paid-meta-advantage-plus, card-paid-google-bidding-recalibration). logged a prediction. sources: search engine land, search engine roundtable, google ads help."}]},{"h":"social — run it today: use meta's edits app as your reels research-and-pitch desk (best-time-to-post, 3-reel side-by-side compare, brand-ready pdf) — plus text-prompt ai restyle","blocks":[{"sub":"meta's free edits app (update jun 17 2026) added audience insights (when your followers are online), side-by-side comparison of up to 3 reels, one-tap export of that data to a pdf for brand/partner pitches, text-prompt ai restyle of clips, and longer exports (up to 15 min on ios)","tags":["social","instagram","reels","meta","edits","analytics","ai-video"],"p":"what changed: on jun 17 2026 meta updated edits — its free, capcut-style mobile video editor wired straight into instagram reels — with features that turn it from an editor into a lightweight analytics-and-pitch tool. the new pieces: (1) audience insights showing when your followers are most active , so you can time posts instead of guessing; (2) compare up to three reels side-by-side in-app to see which hook, length, or format actually performed; (3) export that comparison to a pdf in one tap to share with brands and partners; (4) ai restyle that changes elements of a clip from a text prompt ; and (5) longer exports — up to 15 minutes on ios (up from 10). why it matters for us: this collapses a multi-tool routine (edit in one app, pull analytics in another, screenshot results into a deck for a brand) into one free app that already lives next to where the content posts. for anyone running a brand's organic reels or pitching influencer/ugc work, the 'best-time-to-post + side-by-side a/b + clean pdf' loop is a real, run-it-today workflow upgrade — and it reinforces our standing lesson: design and time content for the signal the platform actually ranks and rewards, then prove the channel with the platform's own numbers.","doit":"(1) install edits (free, ios/android) and connect your instagram account. (2) check audience insights to find your followers' peak-active window, and schedule/post your best cut into that slot. (3) run a real a/b: pick your last three reels (or three variants of one idea) and use the side-by-side compare to see which hook/length/format won — then make more of the winner. (4) export the comparison to a pdf for brand pitches or internal reporting instead of stitching screenshots. (5) try ai restyle on a clip via a text prompt for fast visual variations — but check it's available in your region and mind the regeneration limit. (6) use the 15-min ios export for longer-form vertical cuts where it fits. card: card-social-edits-creator-analytics.","note":"corroboration: reported (2) — social media today and social samosa independently detail the jun 17 analytics + ai-restyle update; techcrunch (jun 11) covers the adjacent edits ai-assistant + desktop expansion. honest scope: ai restyle is not available in all regions and caps how many regenerations you can apply ; the 15-min export is ios-first; some previewed items (ai assistant, desktop) were shown at an invite-only creator event and aren't all shipped. advances the social 'design and measure for the signal the platform ranks on' thread (related: card-social-instagram-your-algorithm, card-social-repurpose-claude). logged a prediction. sources: social media today, social samosa, techcrunch."}]},{"h":"news — the ai landscape (cross-checked; us markets were closed fri jun 19 for juneteenth, so the last close is thu jun 18)","blocks":[{"sub":"norway imposes a near-ban on generative ai in elementary school (grades 1–7) from late august; ages 14–16 only with teacher supervision","tags":["news","ai-tooling","regulation"],"p":"norway's prime minister announced (fri jun 19 ) a near- ban on generative ai for elementary pupils — grades 1–7 (ages 6–13) — and restricted use for ages 14–16 to teacher-supervised only, effective from the new school year in late august . the rationale, amid declining test scores (norway already banned smartphones in schools in 2024): kids must learn to read, write and do math without skipping steps. why it matters / what to expect: a clear regulatory marker that the 'ai everywhere by default' narrative now has formal counter-pressure in education — watch for other european education ministries to follow, and for ed-tech to pivot toward supervised/age-gated ai. confirmed across outlets. sources: reuters, engadget."},{"sub":"apple opens ios to third-party app stores in brazil (ios 26.5, cade deal); elastic to buy deductive ai for up to ~$85m; korea's chip-bonus boom puts the bank of korea on inflation alert","tags":["news","markets","m-and-a","big-tech"],"p":" apple → brazil (confirmed): effective with ios 26.5 under a deal with regulator cade, apple is opening ios to third-party app stores and external payments in brazil — but with a 5% core technology fee , mandatory notarization, no open-web sideloading, and a jul 6 deadline for developers to accept the new license. another dma-style crack in the walled garden; watch for more jurisdictions to extract the same terms. elastic → deductive ai (reported): elastic agreed to acquire crv-backed deductive ai (ai for incident investigation/root-cause) for up to ~ $85m — observability vendors keep buying ai-debugging capability. korea memory cycle (single-source, directional): massive bonuses for samsung/sk hynix chip workers have the bank of korea on inflation alert — a read on how hot the ai-hardware/memory super-cycle is running (dram prices are surging). cross-check any figure against a 2nd source before quoting to a client. sources: engadget, techcrunch, techmeme, cnbc."}]}]

title: Daily Briefing — Friday, June 19
